META.subject: Physics
META.subtitleDoc: CBE Phenomenon-Driven Lesson Sequence — Sub-Strand 1.5 (6 Lessons)

UNIT.subject: Physics
UNIT.content:
• Turning effect of a force (moment of a force)
• The Principle of Moments
• Conditions for equilibrium of a body under parallel forces
• Centre of gravity and its determination for regular and irregular objects
• Stability and states of equilibrium (stable, unstable, and neutral)
• Factors affecting stability: base area and position of centre of gravity
• Application of moments and equilibrium in everyday life (see-saws, cranes, human body posture, Maasai warriors carrying loads)
UNIT.learningOutcomes:
By the end of the sub-strand, the learner should be able to:
a) define the moment of a force and calculate moments in given situations,
b) state and apply the Principle of Moments to solve problems involving balanced systems,
c) state the conditions necessary for a body to be in equilibrium under the action of parallel forces,
d) determine the centre of gravity of regular and irregular objects experimentally,
e) explain the three states of equilibrium (stable, unstable, and neutral) with relevant examples,
f) investigate how the base area and position of the centre of gravity affect the stability of objects,
g) apply the concepts of moments and equilibrium to explain real-life situations such as the design of cranes, double-decker buses, and human body mechanics,
h) appreciate the role of moments and equilibrium in the safe design of structures and everyday objects.
UNIT.coreCompetencies:
• Communication and Collaboration: the learner engages in group investigations (e.g., balancing metre rules, locating centre of gravity of cut-out shapes) and presents findings to the class, practising respectful scientific discourse and shared decision-making.
• Critical Thinking and Problem Solving: the learner analyses why the Nyayo Bus topples on a slope or why a Maasai warrior adjusts posture when carrying a load, constructing logical arguments linking centre of gravity, base area, and moment calculations.
• Learning to Learn: the learner reflects on predictions made before experiments (predict-observe-explain cycle) and revises mental models of balance and stability as new evidence emerges across all six lessons.
• Digital Literacy: the learner uses digital simulations or video clips (e.g., slow-motion footage of the Fundi balancing a heavy roof truss) to gather evidence that would be impractical to obtain in the classroom, interpreting digital data to support scientific explanations.
UNIT.values:
• Responsibility: the learner takes assigned roles during group experiments (measurer, recorder, spokesperson) and ensures equipment is handled safely and returned in good order, reflecting personal accountability.
• Integrity: the learner records experimental results honestly, including unexpected data, and does not alter readings to match predictions, upholding scientific honesty.
• Respect: the learner listens to and builds on the contributions of peers from different backgrounds during class discussions on how communities in Kenya (e.g., tea pickers in Kericho, matatu touts loading cargo) intuitively apply moments in daily work.
• Unity: the learner collaborates across gender and ability lines during practical activities, recognising that safe, balanced structures benefit the whole community.
UNIT.sep:
• SEP 1 – Asking Questions and Defining Problems: students generate and post questions on the Driving Question Board after observing the anchoring phenomenon (a Jiko stool toppling vs. standing), framing what they need to investigate about forces, balance, and stability.
• SEP 2 – Developing and Using Models: students build and iteratively revise a diagrammatic model (free-body / moment-arm diagram) across all six lessons, adding centre-of-gravity markers, moment arrows, and stability annotations as understanding deepens.
• SEP 3 – Planning and Carrying Out Investigations: students design the procedure for locating the centre of gravity of irregular cardboard cut-outs and for verifying the Principle of Moments using a metre rule, masses, and a pivot — deciding variables, controls, and data-collection methods.
• SEP 4 – Analysing and Interpreting Data: students calculate clockwise and anti-clockwise moments from tabulated experimental data, identify patterns, and evaluate whether their results support or contradict the Principle of Moments.
• SEP 6 – Constructing Explanations: students use moment calculations and centre-of-gravity diagrams to explain why a loaded mkokoteni (handcart) tips over when the load is placed too far forward, connecting mathematical evidence to real-world observations.
• SEP 8 – Obtaining, Evaluating, and Communicating Information: students read case-study cards on crane design and tower-crane accidents in Nairobi construction sites, evaluate the reliability of the sources, and communicate findings through annotated diagrams and oral presentations.
UNIT.pcis:
• Safety and Accident Prevention: learners connect the Principle of Moments to vehicle safety — exploring why overloaded matatus and top-heavy lorries on Kenyan highways are prone to rolling, reinforcing road-safety consciousness.
• Environmental Education: learners examine how improper loading of fishing boats on Lake Victoria shifts the centre of gravity dangerously, linking physics to the real environmental and human cost of capsizing accidents.
• Gender Disparity: learners challenge stereotypes by studying contributions of women engineers and physicists (e.g., women working in Nairobi's construction and design sector) who apply moment principles daily, broadening perceptions of who does physics.
• Life Skills — Critical Thinking and Problem Solving: learners evaluate everyday scenarios (carrying water jerricans, stacking sacks of maize) and propose physics-informed improvements, practising decision-making skills transferable beyond the classroom.
UNIT.careers:
• Civil and Structural Engineer: uses moment calculations to design safe beams, bridges (e.g., the Thika Superhighway interchange), and building foundations that remain in equilibrium under load.
• Mechanical Engineer / Crane Operator: applies the Principle of Moments to determine safe lifting loads and boom angles for tower cranes used on Nairobi high-rise construction sites.
• Orthopaedic Physiotherapist / Sports Scientist: analyses how the human body's centre of gravity shifts during running (relevant to Kenyan elite marathon athletes) or injury rehabilitation, using moment principles to design corrective exercises.
• Automotive / Transport Engineer: designs vehicle suspension and load-distribution systems to keep centre of gravity low and prevent rollover — directly relevant to matatu and bus safety standards in Kenya.
• Aviation Technician / Pilot: applies equilibrium and moment principles to aircraft weight-and-balance calculations, a safety-critical skill for pilots operating from Wilson Airport, Nairobi.
• Industrial / Ergonomics Designer: optimises the design of tools, furniture, and farm equipment (e.g., ox-plough balance) to reduce worker strain by aligning forces with comfortable moment arms.
UNIT.focus: This unit investigates how and why objects balance, tip, or remain stable — anchored in the everyday Kenyan experience of objects toppling or staying upright. Students build understanding progressively: from the turning effect of a single force, through the Principle of Moments and conditions for equilibrium, to the role of centre of gravity in determining stability states. A phenomena-driven, investigation-centred approach is used throughout, with students revising a cumulative diagrammatic model in every lesson so that mathematical concepts (moment = F × d) are always connected to visible, meaningful physical behaviour.
UNIT.phenomenon:
ANCHORING PHENOMENON — "The Toppling Jiko Stool"

A three-legged wooden Jiko stool (commonly found in Kenyan homes and market stalls) stands perfectly steady on a flat floor even when a heavy sufuria of ugali is placed near its centre. Yet when a student places a full 20-litre jerrycan of water on one corner of the stool and gently nudges it, the stool topples dramatically. Strikingly, a wider, lower four-legged stool with the same jerrycan placed at the same corner barely tilts — it simply rocks and returns to its upright position. Students observe that the height of the load, the width of the base, and where the load sits all seem to matter — but they cannot yet explain WHY in precise physical terms. This is puzzling because the same force (the weight of the jerrycan) causes toppling in one case and stability in another, suggesting that something about the geometry of the object — not just the force — controls whether it falls. The phenomenon hooks students into wanting to understand turning effects of forces, centre of gravity, and the conditions for equilibrium.
UNIT.supportingPhenomena:
• The Leaning Sugarcane Stack: At a Kisumu market, tall bundles of sugarcane leaning at a steep angle suddenly crash over while shorter, wider bundles remain stable — prompting questions about how the height of the centre of gravity relative to the base governs toppling.
• The Balanced Metre Rule: A uniform metre rule balanced on a pencil pivot shifts dramatically when a 50 g mass (a small stone) is moved just 5 cm further from the pivot — a precise, classroom-observable event that makes the idea of a "turning effect depending on distance" concrete and measurable.
• The Swaying Double-Decker Bus: A KBS double-decker bus navigating a sharp roundabout in Nairobi visibly leans outward; students observe (via video clip) that it does not tip — raising questions about how engineers keep the centre of gravity low enough for the bus to remain in equilibrium through the turn.
• The Tightrope Acrobat's Pole: A circus acrobat at a Nairobi cultural festival carries a long, heavy pole held low and horizontal while walking a wire — counterintuitively, the pole makes balance easier, not harder, linking to the idea of deliberately lowering and widening the effective centre-of-gravity system to increase stability.
UNIT.drivingQuestion:
DRIVING QUESTION: Why do some objects stay balanced while others topple — and how can we use the physics of turning forces to predict and control whether an object will be stable or fall?

KICD KEY INQUIRY QUESTIONS:
1. How is Physics relevant in day-to-day life?
2. How do forces determine the stability and equilibrium of objects we encounter every day?
UNIT.storylineThread:
Lesson 1 — "Why Did the Stool Fall?" (Moment of a Force)
Students observe the anchoring phenomenon (Jiko stool toppling vs. staying upright) and record initial predictions. They are introduced to the concept of the turning effect of a force — the moment — and derive the formula Moment = Force × Perpendicular Distance. They calculate moments for simple scenarios (e.g., pushing open a school gate at different distances from the hinge) and post their first "I notice / I wonder" questions on the Driving Question Board. The cumulative model begins: a diagram of the stool with force arrows.

Lesson 2 — "What Makes a See-Saw Balance?" (Principle of Moments)
Students investigate the Principle of Moments experimentally using a metre rule, a pencil pivot, and improvised masses (bags of beans, coins). They tabulate clockwise and anti-clockwise moments, identify the pattern, and state the Principle. They apply it to solve quantitative problems including a traditional Kenyan see-saw (bao plank across a log). The cumulative model gains moment-arm annotations and a balance condition label.

Lesson 3 — "Where Is the 'Balance Point' Hiding?" (Centre of Gravity — Regular and Irregular Objects)
Students determine the centre of gravity of regular shapes (ruler, rectangular cardboard) by calculation and of irregular shapes (cut-out map of Kenya, oddly shaped plywood) by the plumb-line suspension method. They link the position of centre of gravity to the balance-point concept established in Lesson 2. The cumulative model gains a centre-of-gravity marker (×) on the stool diagram.

Lesson 4 — "Stable, Unstable, or Neutral — How Do We Decide?" (States of Equilibrium)
Students test objects of different shapes and base widths (a tall thin bottle, a wide flat tin, a ball, a cone) by tilting them to small angles and observing whether they return, continue to fall, or stay tilted. They classify each as stable, unstable, or neutral and construct explanations linking each state to the vertical line through the centre of gravity and the base of support. The anchoring phenomenon is revisited: students now explain the stool's behaviour in these terms. The cumulative model gains stability-state annotations.

Lesson 5 — "How Do Engineers Keep Things From Falling?" (Factors Affecting Stability and Real-Life Applications)
Students investigate how lowering the centre of gravity (adding clay to the base of a tall cardboard box) and widening the base (adjusting cardboard flaps) each independently increase stability. They connect findings to real engineering contexts: crane design, double-decker bus geometry, and why maize sacks are stacked in an interlocking pattern. A case-study card on a Nairobi construction-site crane accident is analysed using moment and stability principles. The cumulative model is updated with stability-factor labels and an engineering example inset.

Lesson 6 — "Can We Explain and Predict Toppling?" (Consolidation, Model Finalisation, and DQB Review)
Students return to the full anchoring phenomenon and all supporting phenomena, using their completed cumulative model to write a full scientific explanation of when and why objects topple or remain stable. The Driving Question Board is reviewed: each posted question is answered with evidence from the unit. Students present their models in groups, peer-assess using a co-constructed rubric, and reflect on career connections. A summative problem set (including a jerrycan-on-stool moment calculation) is completed individually to assess mastery of learning outcomes.

LESSONS.title: Why Did the Stool Fall? — Introducing the Moment of a Force

slo.purpose: To launch the anchoring phenomenon and introduce the concept of the moment of a force as a turning effect, anchoring all future learning in the question of why the Jiko stool topples.
slo.knowledge: By the end of this lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) define the moment of a force as the product of the force and the perpendicular distance from the pivot; (b) identify the pivot, force, and perpendicular distance in everyday Kenyan scenarios; (c) state that the moment of a force is measured in Newton-metres (N·m).
slo.skills: By the end of this lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) measure and record force and distance values to calculate moments; (b) use a simple beam-balance apparatus to observe turning effects; (c) construct an initial annotated sketch-model of the stool phenomenon showing force and pivot.
slo.attitudes: The learner appreciates that Physics principles — particularly turning effects of forces — are embedded in everyday Kenyan objects such as the Jiko stool, sufuria, and jerrycan, and begins to develop curiosity about predicting stability.
slo.keyInquiry: How do forces determine the stability and equilibrium of objects we encounter every day?
slo.purposeInStoryline: This is Lesson 1 of the Moments of Equilibrium storyline. It launches the anchoring phenomenon — the toppling Jiko stool — and opens the Driving Question Board. Students make their first predictions about why the stool topples, gather initial observational evidence about turning effects, and build a preliminary model. Every subsequent lesson will return to this stool scenario to deepen the explanation.
slo.safetyNotes: Ensure the demonstration stool is placed on a stable, clear surface away from the edges of desks. The 20-litre jerrycan used in demonstration must be handled by the teacher only — not lifted by learners. Warn students to stand back (at least 1 metre) during the toppling demonstration. No glass apparatus in this lesson. Learners handling metre rules and masses should be reminded not to drop masses on feet.

LESSONS.overview: Students encounter the anchoring phenomenon for the first time: a three-legged Jiko stool that stands firm under a sufuria of ugali but topples when a full 20-litre jerrycan is placed at its corner. Through structured prediction, a live teacher demonstration, a hands-on beam-balance activity, and guided sensemaking, students discover that forces create turning effects that depend on BOTH the size of the force AND the distance from a pivot. The lesson closes by opening the class Driving Question Board (DQB) with student-generated questions and an initial sketch-model of the stool phenomenon. No full explanation is reached — the mystery deepens purposefully.

framework.learnerExperience:
Students enter to find a three-legged Jiko stool placed at the front of the room with a sufuria (empty) resting near its centre. Without any introduction, they are handed a half-page 'Prediction Slip' and asked to silently read two scenario cards:
• CARD A: 'A full sufuria of ugali (3 kg) is placed at the centre of the stool. Does it topple?'
• CARD B: 'A full 20-litre jerrycan (20 kg) is placed on one corner of the same stool. A student gives it a gentle sideways nudge. Does it topple?'
Students write their prediction (YES / NO / MAYBE), draw a quick force-arrow sketch on the stool diagram provided, and write ONE reason for their prediction. They then share predictions with a shoulder partner (Think-Pair) before three pairs share aloud to the class.
framework.teacherMoves:
1. Before class: set up the Jiko stool at the front. Place the empty sufuria on it. Write on the board: 'PHENOMENON: Why did the stool fall?' Do NOT explain anything yet.
2. Distribute Prediction Slips as students enter. Say verbatim: 'Do not talk yet — read both cards and write your prediction silently. You have 3 minutes.'
3. After 3 minutes, say: 'Now turn to your shoulder partner — you have 2 minutes. Share your prediction and your reason. Try to agree or find where you disagree.'
4. After pair discussion, cold-call exactly 3 pairs using a random name stick. For each pair ask: 'What did you predict — and what was your reason?' [WAIT TIME: 10 seconds after each question before accepting an answer.]
5. Record the three predictions on the board in a T-chart labelled 'TOPPLES / DOES NOT TOPPLE'. Do NOT affirm or correct any prediction. Say: 'Hold these ideas — we will come back to test them very soon.'
6. Ask the whole class: 'What would you NEED to know or measure to be sure of your prediction?' Cold-call 2 students. [WAIT TIME: 12 seconds.] Write their responses under the T-chart as 'We need to find out…'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Think-Pair-Share with Prediction Slip — this strategy activates prior knowledge about forces and balance (from Grade 8 science), reveals existing misconceptions (e.g. 'heavier objects always topple'), and creates cognitive dissonance that motivates the rest of the lesson. Recording predictions publicly ensures accountability and allows the teacher to track conceptual change across the lesson.
framework.formativeAssessment: Collect Prediction Slips at the end of the phase. Observable indicators: (a) student has drawn at least one force arrow on the stool diagram; (b) student has given a reason that references either the weight, the position of the load, or the shape of the stool — even if imprecise. Flag slips where the student has given no reason or written only 'I don't know' for targeted scaffolding during Phase 3.

framework.learnerExperience:
PART A — Live Demonstration (10 min): The teacher performs a two-part live demonstration at the front.
• Demo 1: Places the empty sufuria at the centre of the Jiko stool — stool stays upright. Places it near one edge — stool leans but does not fall.
• Demo 2: Replaces the sufuria with a filled 20-litre jerrycan placed on one corner of the stool, then gives the stool a gentle nudge — the stool topples dramatically.
• Demo 3: Moves the jerrycan to the wider, lower four-legged stool. Nudges it — the stool rocks and self-rights.
Students complete an Observation Table in their notebooks with columns: OBJECT | WHERE LOAD IS PLACED | HEIGHT OF STOOL | NUMBER OF LEGS | RESULT (topple / stable).

PART B — Beam-Balance Turning Activity (10 min): Each group of 4 receives: 1 × 30 cm wooden ruler balanced on a pencil (acting as a pivot at the centre), 3 × 50g masses, 1 × metre rule for measuring distances.
Task: Place one 50g mass at 5 cm from the pivot on the LEFT side. Observe. Now place a second 50g mass at 10 cm from the pivot on the RIGHT side. Observe. Record what happens in each case. Groups then try to find a position for a single 50g mass on the right that balances a 100g mass placed 6 cm to the left. Record distances and masses used.
framework.teacherMoves:
PART A:
1. Before Demo 2, say verbatim: 'Watch carefully — I want you to notice exactly WHEN the stool starts to move and WHERE the jerrycan is sitting.' [Place jerrycan, step back, give a single gentle push.]
2. After the stool topples, say: 'Turn to your table group — 60 seconds — tell each other: what EXACTLY caused it to fall? Was it just the weight? Or was something else involved?' [WAIT TIME: allow full 60 seconds of group talk.]
3. Cold-call 2 groups for their observation (NOT explanation yet). [WAIT TIME: 10 seconds.]
4. Say: 'Write your observations in your table — not your explanation yet. Observations only.'

PART B:
1. Circulate to each group during the beam-balance activity. At each group, ask: 'Which side is turning? What TWO things are you changing when you move the mass further out?' [WAIT TIME: 10 seconds at each group.]
2. If a group finds a balance position, ask: 'Can you see any pattern between the numbers on each side? Multiply the mass by the distance — what do you get on each side?' [Do NOT give the formula — let groups discover it.]
3. With 2 minutes remaining, say: 'Freeze. Write down in one sentence what you think the TURNING EFFECT depends on.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Structured Observation Table + Hands-On Discovery — the two-part observe phase separates macroscopic phenomenon observation (stool demonstration) from quantitative pattern-finding (beam-balance). This dual-encoding approach ensures students connect the dramatic visual phenomenon to measurable variables before any formula is introduced. The beam-balance activity embeds NGSS Science & Engineering Practice 3 (Planning and Carrying Out Investigations) and Practice 4 (Analysing and Interpreting Data).
framework.formativeAssessment: Circulate and check Observation Tables during Part A. Observable indicators: (a) student correctly records at least 3 rows in the observation table; (b) student distinguishes between 'where the load is placed' and 'how heavy the load is' as separate variables. During Part B, listen for groups saying words like 'the further out, the more turning' — this indicates readiness for the moment formula in Phase 3. Flag groups that only record 'it balanced' without recording the distances.

framework.learnerExperience:
PART A — Concept Introduction via Guided Notes (8 min): Students open their ARES platform Guided Notes page for Lesson 1. The teacher leads a structured explanation:
• Define MOMENT OF A FORCE: 'A moment is the turning effect of a force about a pivot point.'
• Formula: Moment (M) = Force (F) × Perpendicular distance from pivot (d) → M = F × d
• Unit: Newton-metre (N·m)
• Students label a diagram showing: the pivot, the line of action of force, and the perpendicular distance — using the Jiko stool as the diagram context.

PART B — Worked Example with Kenyan Context (5 min): Teacher works through: 'A sufuria containing 3 kg of ugali (weight = 30 N) is placed 0.2 m from the leg of a Jiko stool acting as the pivot. Calculate the moment of the force about that leg.'
M = F × d = 30 N × 0.2 m = 6 N·m

PART C — Apply: Return to Beam-Balance Data (7 min): Groups retrieve their beam-balance data from Phase 2. They now calculate the moment on each side using M = F × d (using weight = mass × 10 N/kg). They check: when balanced, are the moments equal on both sides? Students write a one-sentence conclusion in their notebooks.
framework.teacherMoves:
PART A:
1. Write 'MOMENT = TURNING EFFECT' in large letters on the board BEFORE defining it formally. Say: 'You have all just observed moments — you just did not have the name for it yet.'
2. Write the formula slowly: M = F × d. Say verbatim: 'The moment is NOT just about how heavy the object is — it also depends on HOW FAR from the pivot the force acts. This is why the jerrycan caused toppling even though the stool could hold the sufuria.'
3. Point back to the stool at the front: 'Where was the pivot in our demonstration?' Cold-call 1 student. [WAIT TIME: 10 seconds.] Confirm: the contact legs of the stool act as the pivot line.

PART B:
1. Write the worked example on the board step by step. After writing M = 30 × 0.2, pause and say: 'Before I multiply — tell your partner: will this moment be bigger or smaller if I move the sufuria to 0.4 m from the pivot? Discuss — 30 seconds.' Cold-call 1 student. [WAIT TIME: 10 seconds.]
2. Complete the calculation. Say: '6 Newton-metres. Write that unit carefully — it is NOT Newtons alone, and it is NOT metres alone. It is BOTH together.'

PART C:
1. Say: 'Go back to your beam-balance data. You now have the tool to check whether the moments balanced. Do the calculation — 5 minutes.'
2. Circulate. If a group's moments do not match (due to measurement error), say: 'Good — science includes measurement uncertainty. What could have caused the difference?' [WAIT TIME: 10 seconds.]
3. Cold-call 2 groups to share their conclusions. Ask: 'Does your conclusion explain why the stool toppled? What was the larger moment in the stool demonstration?'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Concept-Before-Formula anchoring with Phenomenon Callback — by naming the concept ('turning effect') before writing the formula, and immediately connecting the formula back to the stool demonstration, students encode M = F × d as a physical meaning rather than an abstract equation. The apply step (Part C) uses NGSS Practice 5 (Using Mathematics and Computational Thinking) to consolidate understanding through their own data.
framework.formativeAssessment: Collect notebooks at the end of Part C (or photograph group work on ARES). Observable indicators: (a) student correctly applies M = F × d with correct units in the worked example; (b) student's beam-balance calculation shows moments within ±1 N·m on both sides for the balanced case; (c) student's one-sentence conclusion references both force AND distance. Students who calculate only force OR only distance without multiplying need immediate re-teaching of the formula meaning.

framework.learnerExperience:
Each student receives two sticky notes (different colours — e.g. yellow for 'I wonder…' questions, pink for 'I now understand…' statements). Students independently write:
• YELLOW sticky: One question they still have about the stool phenomenon OR about moments/stability (e.g. 'Why does a wider base make it harder to topple?', 'Does the height of the jerrycan matter?', 'What is centre of gravity?').
• PINK sticky: One thing they now understand that they did NOT understand at the start of the lesson.
Students walk to the classroom DQB (a large sheet of paper or whiteboard section) and place their stickies. The teacher facilitates a 3-minute gallery reading — students silently read the board. The teacher then clusters the yellow questions into 3–4 theme groups in front of the class and labels each cluster (e.g. 'Cluster 1: Where is the balance point?', 'Cluster 2: How does height matter?', 'Cluster 3: What makes something stable?'). These clusters will be revisited at the start of every subsequent lesson.
framework.teacherMoves:
1. Distribute sticky notes. Say verbatim: 'The yellow note is for questions you STILL HAVE — things the stool phenomenon has made you wonder about. The pink note is one thing you now understand. You have 3 minutes — write independently, not with your partner.'
2. Invite students to the DQB one row at a time. Say: 'Place your stickies anywhere on the board — we will organise them together.'
3. After gallery walk, say: 'I can see some of your questions are about the same big ideas. Watch as I group them.' Physically move stickies into clusters in front of students, narrating: 'These three questions all seem to be about WHERE forces act — I'll call this cluster 'Location of Force.' These are about something called centre of gravity — we will get there in Lesson 3.'
4. Point to the clusters and say: 'This board is our science story. By the time we finish Sub-Strand 1.5, every cluster on this board should have an answer. Your job — and my job — is to fill this board with understanding, not just facts.'
5. Photograph the DQB for the ARES platform record. Cold-call 1 student: 'Which cluster do you most want to answer first — and why?' [WAIT TIME: 12 seconds.]
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Driving Question Board (DQB) with Question Clustering — the DQB is a metacognitive tool (NGSS Practice 8: Obtaining, Evaluating, and Communicating Information) that externalises student thinking, tracks conceptual growth across the unit, and gives students ownership of the inquiry storyline. Clustering questions publicly shows students that science is organised around big ideas, not isolated facts.
framework.formativeAssessment: Review all yellow sticky notes after class. Observable indicators: (a) at least 70% of students have written a genuine science question (not 'Will we do an experiment?'); (b) questions referencing 'centre of gravity', 'base width', 'height of load', or 'balance point' signal that Phase 2 successfully surfaced the key variables; (c) pink notes where students write 'I now understand moments = force × distance' confirm Phase 3 success. Collect photos of DQB as a class-level formative artefact.

framework.learnerExperience:
Students open a blank 'Model Builder' page (ARES platform or exercise book). Using the stool demonstration as context, each student draws an initial annotated model of WHY the stool toppled. The model must include:
1. A side-view sketch of the Jiko stool with the jerrycan on one corner.
2. A labelled downward force arrow (Weight of jerrycan, F = 200 N) at the position of the jerrycan.
3. A labelled pivot point (the contact leg on the opposite side).
4. A labelled perpendicular distance (d) from the pivot to the line of action of the force.
5. A calculated moment value (M = F × d using estimated or given values).
6. One sentence: 'The stool toppled because…'
Students share their model with their group. Groups identify ONE thing all their models have in common and ONE thing that differs. One spokesperson reports to the class. Teacher notes areas of disagreement on the board as 'Model Revision Points — revisit in Lesson 2'.
framework.teacherMoves:
1. Say verbatim: 'Scientists build models to explain what they observe. Your model today does NOT need to be perfect — it needs to show your BEST THINKING right now. We will improve it in every lesson.'
2. Write the model requirements on the board as a checklist (items 1–6 above). Say: 'You have 7 minutes to draw your model independently.'
3. After 7 minutes, say: 'Compare with your group — 3 minutes. Find one agreement and one disagreement in your models.'
4. Cold-call 2 group spokespeople. [WAIT TIME: 10 seconds each.] For each report, ask: 'What caused the disagreement in your group — was it about the position of the pivot, or the direction of the force, or something else?'
5. Write disagreements on the board under 'Model Revision Points'. Say: 'These disagreements are EXACTLY what science looks like. We will resolve each of these in the coming lessons. Do not erase your model — we will look back at it at the end of the sub-strand to see how your thinking has changed.'
6. Close the lesson by pointing back to the stool: 'We now know that the jerrycan created a moment — a turning effect — about the pivot leg. But we still cannot fully explain WHY the four-legged stool did not topple. What are we missing? That question will drive Lessons 2 and 3.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Initial Model Construction with Group Comparison — initial model building (NGSS Practice 2: Developing and Using Models) makes student thinking visible and creates a baseline artefact for tracking conceptual change. Group comparison surfaces productive disagreements (e.g. different students placing the pivot at different legs) that create the intellectual need for more precise understanding. Publicly recording 'Model Revision Points' institutionalises the idea that models evolve with evidence.
framework.formativeAssessment: Collect or photograph models before students leave. Observable indicators: (a) student has correctly placed the force arrow acting downward at the position of the jerrycan (not at the centre of the stool); (b) student has identified a pivot leg — even if not yet the correct one — showing understanding that a pivot is needed for a moment; (c) student has written a moment calculation with correct units (N·m); (d) the 'The stool toppled because…' sentence references both force and distance rather than weight alone. Students who show only a weight arrow with no pivot or distance label require re-engagement with the M = F × d concept in Lesson 2.

LESSONS.teacherReflection:
After the lesson, reflect on the following questions:
1. PREDICTION PHASE: Did students' initial predictions reveal specific misconceptions (e.g. 'only heavy objects topple', 'all three-legged stools are unstable')? Which misconceptions were most common — and how will you address them explicitly in Lesson 2?
2. OBSERVATION PHASE: Did the beam-balance activity surface the pattern M = F × d independently, or did groups need significant prompting? If most groups needed prompting, consider adding a data table with a pre-filled 'Force × Distance' column in Lesson 2 activities.
3. EXPLAIN PHASE: Were students able to connect the abstract formula M = F × d back to the physical stool? Listen for language in Phase 3 cold-calls — students saying 'the jerrycan was far from the pivot' show strong conceptual linkage; students saying only 'the jerrycan was heavy' need the distance component reinforced.
4. DQB QUALITY: Did the DQB generate questions about centre of gravity, base width, or load height? If students' questions remained only about 'why does it fall', the phenomenon may need a more explicit second demonstration in Lesson 2 highlighting the four-legged stool contrast.
5. MODEL BUILDING: What were the most common 'Model Revision Points' identified during Phase 5? Use these to sequence the focus of Lessons 2 and 3. For example, if most disagreements were about pivot location, make pivot identification the opening activity of Lesson 2.
6. PACING: Was 80 minutes sufficient? If Phase 2 (beam-balance) overran, consider pre-assembling the rulers and masses before class, or reducing the number of balance configurations from 3 to 2.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: A three-legged Jiko stool with a sufuria of ugali at its centre remained stable, but toppled when a full 20-litre jerrycan was placed at one corner and nudged. A wider, lower four-legged stool with the same jerrycan simply rocked and self-righted. In the beam-balance activity, moving a mass further from the pivot caused greater turning, and two different masses could balance when placed at specific distances on opposite sides.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: A force acting at a distance from a pivot creates a MOMENT (turning effect). The moment of a force is calculated as M = F × d (force multiplied by perpendicular distance from the pivot) and is measured in Newton-metres (N·m). The jerrycan created a large moment about the stool's pivot leg because of its large weight AND its distance from that leg — not weight alone.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: The stool toppled because the jerrycan's weight (200 N) acting at a large perpendicular distance from the pivot leg created a large clockwise moment (M = F × d) that was not balanced by any opposing moment. The four-legged stool did not topple under the same conditions — suggesting that something about the geometry (width of base, position of pivot legs) affects whether the turning effect causes permanent toppling or is resisted. This 'something' — involving centre of gravity and conditions for equilibrium — remains to be explained in Lessons 2 and 3.

LESSONS.aresKeywords: moment of a force, turning effect, pivot, perpendicular distance, Newton-metre, Jiko stool, equilibrium, toppling, beam balance, stability, M = F × d, Driving Question Board, anchoring phenomenon, moments and equilibrium, Grade 10 Physics Kenya CBE

LESSONS.title: Turning Forces: Introducing the Moment of a Force

slo.purpose: To introduce the concept of the moment (turning effect) of a force, derive the formula Moment = Force × Perpendicular Distance, and apply it to simple real-life Kenyan scenarios, connecting the turning effect back to why the Jiko stool topples.
slo.knowledge: Learners will know that: (a) a force applied at a distance from a pivot produces a turning effect called a moment; (b) the moment of a force is calculated as Moment = Force × Perpendicular Distance from the pivot; (c) the SI unit of moment is the Newton-metre (N·m); (d) increasing either the force or the perpendicular distance increases the moment.
slo.skills: Learners will be able to: (a) measure and record perpendicular distances from a pivot in a physical setup; (b) calculate moments using the formula M = F × d; (c) compare moments in different physical configurations; (d) represent turning forces with annotated force-arrow diagrams; (e) post structured 'I Notice / I Wonder' questions on the Driving Question Board.
slo.attitudes: Learners will appreciate that: (a) precise measurement and careful observation are essential to understanding physical phenomena; (b) Physics concepts such as moments are embedded in everyday Kenyan objects and tools; (c) collaborative discussion deepens individual understanding.
slo.keyInquiry: How do forces determine the stability and equilibrium of objects we encounter every day?
slo.purposeInStoryline: In Lesson 1 students observed the Jiko stool phenomenon and made initial predictions about why the stool topples. In this lesson (Lesson 2 of 6) students gather the first piece of explanatory evidence: the idea that a force's turning effect depends not only on its magnitude but on WHERE it is applied relative to the pivot. This directly begins to explain why placing the jerrycan at the corner (far from the centre) has a greater toppling effect than placing it near the middle. The concept of the moment is the foundational building block — all subsequent lessons on the principle of moments, centre of gravity, and equilibrium conditions will rest on this lesson's derivation and formula.
slo.safetyNotes: Ensure metre rules and masses are handled carefully to avoid dropping on feet. If a heavy sufuria or jerrycan is used as a demonstration prop, place it on a stable surface and never drop it. Students must remain seated during the teacher demonstration of the tilting stool to avoid injury if the stool tips. Wet floors near demonstration tables should be dried immediately.

LESSONS.overview: Students re-engage with the anchoring phenomenon (the toppling Jiko stool) by revisiting their Lesson 1 predictions. The teacher demonstrates that the same force (weight of a jerrycan) has a dramatically different toppling effect depending on where it is applied relative to the stool's supporting legs. Through a guided hands-on activity using a metre rule balanced on a pencil pivot, students discover that moving a 500 g mass further from the pivot makes it harder to balance — i.e., the turning effect grows with distance. The class derives the formula Moment = Force × Perpendicular Distance (M = F × d), calculates moments for Kenyan-context scenarios (school gate hinges, a mama pushing a mkokoteni cart), and posts structured 'I Notice / I Wonder' questions on the Driving Question Board. The lesson closes with students adding the first annotated layer — force arrows showing moments — to their cumulative model diagram of the Jiko stool.

framework.learnerExperience: Students open their exercise books to their Lesson 1 prediction diagrams (the Jiko stool with the jerrycan at the corner vs. the centre). In pairs, they discuss: 'Your Lesson 1 prediction said the stool topples because of [their reason]. Today we are going to test that idea. Revise or defend your prediction: does the POSITION of the jerrycan change how hard the stool is to topple, and WHY?' Each student individually writes a revised prediction in one sentence, then shares with their partner. Four cold-called pairs share aloud. The teacher records key prediction vocabulary on the board in two columns: FORCE-BASED reasons (e.g., 'the jerrycan is heavy') vs. POSITION-BASED reasons (e.g., 'it is placed too far from the middle'). Students are told: 'By the end of today you will be able to say exactly HOW position changes the toppling effect — using a number and a formula.'
framework.teacherMoves:
1. Display the Lesson 1 phenomenon image (or re-place the stool and jerrycan prop on the demonstration bench). Say verbatim: 'Cast your minds back to our Jiko stool. Last lesson you predicted WHY it topples. I want you to revisit that prediction now — silently, in your book — for 90 seconds.' [WAIT 90 seconds — circulate, do NOT prompt.]
2. Say: 'Now discuss with your partner: does WHERE the jerrycan sits on the stool change the toppling effect? Give a reason.' [WAIT 2 minutes for pair talk.]
3. Cold-call exactly 4 pairs (choose 1 force-based, 1 position-based, 1 mixed, 1 uncertain). After each: 'Thank you. Class, do you agree or disagree? Thumbs up or down.' [WAIT 10 seconds for thumbs.]
4. Annotate the board columns. Say verbatim: 'Notice we have two types of ideas — force-based and position-based. Today we will find out which one Physics says is the key to the toppling puzzle — or whether it is BOTH.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Strategy: PREDICTION REVISION (Elicit-Confront-Resolve). Students surface their prior mental models before instruction. Sorting predictions into 'force-based' vs. 'position-based' columns creates a visible cognitive conflict — students see that their class has two competing explanations. This tension motivates the evidence-gathering activity that follows and gives the teacher diagnostic insight into which misconceptions (e.g., 'it topples just because the jerrycan is heavy') need to be directly addressed.
framework.formativeAssessment: OBSERVABLE INDICATOR: After the 4 cold-calls, at least 60% of students should be able to state that BOTH the magnitude of the force AND its position relative to the pivot seem to matter (thumbs up for position-based explanations). If fewer than half agree, the teacher pauses and restates: 'Look at the stool — if I put the same jerrycan right in the CENTRE, does it topple? [No.] But at the corner? [Yes.] Same weight — different position. Write that in your book as a KEY OBSERVATION.' This is a critical gate before advancing to Phase 2.

framework.learnerExperience: Groups of 3–4 students receive: one metre rule, one pencil (acts as the pivot/fulcrum placed at the 50 cm mark), one 500 g mass, and a printed data table. Task: Place the 500 g mass at 10 cm from the pivot, then 20 cm, 30 cm, 40 cm, and 50 cm from the pivot. At each position, students observe whether the rule tips and record in the table: (a) the distance from pivot in cm, (b) a qualitative observation (balanced / tips slightly / tips strongly), and (c) a sketch showing the mass position and the direction the rule tips. After completing the table, each group answers on a sticky note: 'What pattern do you notice between distance and tipping?' Groups stick their sticky notes on the class 'Evidence Wall.' The teacher then performs a brief dramatic demonstration: pushing open the school's gate (or a classroom door) near the hinge vs. near the outer edge, asking 'Which feels HARDER? Why?'
framework.teacherMoves:
1. Distribute equipment. Say verbatim: 'You have 12 minutes. Your job is NOT to explain yet — just observe and record carefully. What changes as you move the mass further from the pivot? Fill in the table. Silence for the first 2 minutes so everyone makes their own first observation.' [WAIT 2 minutes — silent individual observation.]
2. After 2 minutes: 'You may now discuss quietly in your group as you continue the experiment.'
3. At 10 minutes: 'Two minutes left — write your pattern statement on your sticky note and post it on the Evidence Wall.'
4. Perform the door/gate demonstration. Ask: 'Raise your hand if it felt harder to push near the hinge.' [WAIT 10 seconds.] Cold-call one student: 'Kwame, describe in your own words WHY you think it felt harder near the hinge.'
5. Say verbatim: 'This is the SAME idea as our metre rule experiment — and the SAME idea as our Jiko stool. The turning effect of a force changes with distance from the pivot. Physicists give this turning effect a name. Any guesses?' [WAIT 15 seconds — accept all guesses without affirming yet.]
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Strategy: STRUCTURED OBSERVATION WITH PATTERN DETECTION. Students generate empirical data (qualitative observations across 5 distance positions) before any formula is introduced. The sticky-note Evidence Wall makes all groups' patterns visible simultaneously, allowing students to see convergent findings across different groups — building confidence that the pattern is real and repeatable. The door demonstration bridges the abstract metre-rule result to a lived Kenyan school experience, reinforcing that this is not a textbook abstraction.
framework.formativeAssessment: OBSERVABLE INDICATOR: Check the sticky notes on the Evidence Wall. Every group should state a pattern equivalent to: 'The further the mass from the pivot, the stronger the tipping effect.' If any group states only 'the mass made it tip' (ignoring distance), the teacher directs them: 'Look at your table — at 10 cm vs. 50 cm, does the mass change? No. What changes?' This is done publicly so all groups benefit. An additional formative check: ask all students to hold up their data tables — scan for complete entries across all 5 distance positions. Incomplete tables indicate a procedural gap that must be closed before Phase 3.

framework.learnerExperience: The teacher leads a whole-class derivation of the moment formula, anchored in the metre rule data. Students follow along in their books, filling in a guided notes frame (printed or dictated). Step 1 — Naming: Students learn the term 'moment of a force' (or 'torque') and its definition: the turning effect produced by a force about a pivot. Step 2 — Formula derivation: using the metre rule data, students observe that doubling the distance doubled the tipping effect → the relationship is proportional → Moment = Force × Perpendicular Distance, or M = F × d. Step 3 — Units: Force in Newtons, distance in metres → moment in Newton-metres (N·m). Step 4 — Worked examples: (a) A mama applies a 40 N force to push a mkokoteni cart handle 0.8 m from the wheel axle pivot — calculate the moment. (b) A student pushes a school gate with 25 N at 1.2 m from the hinge — calculate the moment. (c) The same student pushes with 25 N at only 0.3 m from the hinge — compare the moments and explain which is easier. Step 5 — Phenomenon reconnection: students look at their Jiko stool diagram and annotate: 'When the jerrycan is at the corner, d is LARGE → moment is LARGE → large turning effect → stool topples.'
framework.teacherMoves:
1. Write on the board: 'MOMENT OF A FORCE' in large letters. Say verbatim: 'This is the Physics name for the turning effect you just discovered. Write this definition word for word: The moment of a force is the product of the force and the perpendicular distance of its line of action from the pivot.'
2. Say: 'From your experiment — when you doubled the distance from 10 cm to 20 cm, what happened to the tipping effect?' [WAIT 10 seconds.] Cold-call 2 students.
3. Write on board: M = F × d. Walk through the derivation verbally: 'Moment equals Force times perpendicular distance. This is our formula for today.'
4. Work Example (a) aloud on the board. Then say: 'Now YOU do Example (b) — solo — 3 minutes. No talking.' [WAIT 3 minutes.] Then: 'Compare your answer with your partner — 1 minute.' Cold-call one pair to share. Correct on board if needed.
5. Say: 'Now look at your Jiko stool diagram from Lesson 1. Where is the jerrycan placed when the stool topples? Use a ruler — estimate the distance from the nearest leg. Write the moment calculation — estimate the force as 200 N (20 kg jerrycan). What moment do you get?' [WAIT 3 minutes individual work.]
6. Verbatim closing statement: 'So the stool does not topple just because the jerrycan is heavy. It topples because the jerrycan is FAR from the pivot — the supporting leg — so the moment is LARGE. This is our first answer to the driving question. Write it in your book as EVIDENCE STATEMENT 1.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Strategy: GUIDED DERIVATION WITH PHENOMENON ANCHORING. Rather than presenting M = F × d as a received formula, the teacher constructs it from the students' own experimental data, making the formula feel discovered rather than imposed. Each worked example uses a Kenyan context (mkokoteni cart, school gate) so the formula immediately feels applicable rather than abstract. The explicit 'EVIDENCE STATEMENT 1' step creates a written knowledge artifact that students will build on in each subsequent lesson — directly connecting new knowledge to the anchoring phenomenon.
framework.formativeAssessment:
OBSERVABLE INDICATOR 1: During solo work on Example (b), circulate and check that students are (i) converting cm to m, (ii) multiplying F × d (not adding), and (iii) writing the unit N·m. A common error is using cm instead of m — address this publicly: 'I am seeing some books with units of N·cm. Is that correct? What is the SI unit of distance?' [WAIT 10 seconds.]
OBSERVABLE INDICATOR 2: The Jiko stool moment calculation should yield approximately 200 N × 0.35 m ≈ 70 N·m (depending on student estimates). Accept any reasonable estimate — the key formative check is whether students correctly identify 'd' as the distance from the jerrycan to the nearest supporting leg (the pivot), not the distance to the centre of the stool. If students use the wrong distance, prompt: 'Where does the stool pivot when it begins to topple? Which leg acts as the fulcrum?'

framework.learnerExperience: Each student individually writes TWO sticky notes: one 'I NOTICE…' and one 'I WONDER…' based on today's lesson AND the anchoring phenomenon. They then read their partner's sticky notes, agree on the best one from each category, and post them on the class DQB under the columns: 'WHAT WE NOW KNOW' and 'QUESTIONS WE STILL HAVE.' The teacher reads aloud 4–5 posted wonders, clusters similar questions, and explicitly labels emerging sub-questions (e.g., 'Several of you are asking: what happens when there are TWO forces turning in OPPOSITE directions? That is what we will investigate in Lesson 3.'). The teacher also reviews any sticky notes from Lesson 1 that today's lesson has now answered, moving them from 'Questions We Have' to 'What We Now Know,' modelling that the DQB is a living record of growing understanding.
framework.teacherMoves:
1. Distribute two sticky notes per student. Say verbatim: 'You have 3 minutes — solo — to write one I NOTICE and one I WONDER. Your I NOTICE must use the word MOMENT. Your I WONDER must be a question you genuinely cannot yet answer.' [WAIT 3 minutes — no talking.]
2. 'Share with your partner — choose the best one from each category — 1 minute.' [WAIT 1 minute.]
3. 'Come post your chosen sticky notes.' [Allow 2 minutes for posting.]
4. Read 4–5 wonders aloud. Say: 'I am seeing a cluster here — three groups are asking about what happens when forces pull in OPPOSITE directions. Excellent — that is exactly where we are heading.' Write the cluster label on the DQB.
5. Move at least one Lesson 1 sticky note to 'What We Now Know.' Say verbatim: 'In Lesson 1, Aisha asked: does WHERE you place the weight matter? Today's formula gives us our answer — yes, and we can now CALCULATE exactly how much it matters. That question is answered. It moves here.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Strategy: DRIVING QUESTION BOARD CURATION. The DQB is not a decoration — it is a publicly visible epistemological record. By explicitly moving answered questions and labelling new clusters, the teacher models scientific knowledge-building as cumulative and self-correcting. Students see their own questions becoming the roadmap for future lessons, which increases ownership and motivation. The constraint ('Your I NOTICE must use the word MOMENT') ensures students encode the lesson's key term in their own language rather than copying a definition.
framework.formativeAssessment: OBSERVABLE INDICATOR: Scan all posted 'I NOTICE' sticky notes before clustering. Every note should reference 'moment' and 'distance' or 'pivot.' Notes that say only 'forces cause turning' (without the distance relationship) indicate that the M = F × d derivation did not fully land. The teacher makes a mental note of the authors of vague notices and will target them in the opening retrieval activity of Lesson 3. 'I WONDER' notes that ask 'what if two forces act at the same time?' or 'what if forces act in opposite directions?' signal readiness for the principle of moments (Lesson 3) — celebrate these publicly as 'great scientific questions.'

framework.learnerExperience: Students turn to their Lesson 1 cumulative model page (a diagram of the Jiko stool with the jerrycan). Using the knowledge from today's lesson, they add a NEW LAYER to the model: (1) Draw and label a force arrow representing the weight of the jerrycan, pointing downward, with its magnitude (200 N). (2) Draw and label a dashed line showing the perpendicular distance 'd' from the force's line of action to the nearest supporting leg (pivot). (3) Write the calculated moment value (in N·m) next to the arrow. (4) Add a caption: 'Large d → Large Moment → Large turning effect → Stool topples.' Students also add a contrasting mini-diagram: the same jerrycan placed at the centre of the stool — shorter d, smaller moment — with the caption 'Small d → Small Moment → Stool stays upright.' At the bottom of the model page, students write their current 'best explanation' of the driving question in 2–3 sentences, acknowledging what they still do not know.
framework.teacherMoves:
1. Say verbatim: 'Open your Lesson 1 model diagram. You are NOT drawing a new diagram — you are ADDING to the same one. Scientists revise their models as they learn more. Today you are adding LAYER 2: moments.' Display a teacher model on the board (pre-drawn stool outline) and annotate live as you give instructions.
2. Step through each annotation aloud: 'First — force arrow on the jerrycan, pointing DOWN, labelled W = 200 N. Second — dashed line from the arrow to the nearest leg. Label it d. Third — write your moment calculation. Fourth — caption.' [WAIT 5 minutes for students to draw.]
3. Say: 'Now add the contrasting diagram — same stool, jerrycan in the CENTRE. Smaller d. What happens to the moment? Calculate it.' [WAIT 3 minutes.]
4. 'Final step — write your 2–3 sentence best explanation of our driving question using the words: moment, force, distance, pivot, topple.' [WAIT 3 minutes.]
5. Cold-call 2 students to read their explanation aloud. After each, ask the class: 'Does this explanation use evidence from our experiment today? Thumbs up or down.' [WAIT 10 seconds for thumbs.]
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Strategy: CUMULATIVE MODEL REVISION (Layered Annotated Diagram). The model is the same diagram across all 6 lessons, with each lesson adding one explanatory layer. This structure makes conceptual growth visible and tangible — students can literally point to what they knew in Lesson 1 vs. what they know now. The contrasting mini-diagram (jerrycan at centre vs. corner) forces students to apply the formula comparatively, not just computationally, deepening conceptual understanding. The 'best explanation so far' paragraph activates metacognition: students must articulate both what they now know AND what they still cannot explain, sustaining inquiry momentum.
framework.formativeAssessment: OBSERVABLE INDICATOR: Collect (or photograph) 6–8 model diagrams at random before students leave. Check for: (a) force arrow pointing downward with N label ✓; (b) dashed perpendicular distance line from arrow to pivot leg ✓; (c) correct M = F × d calculation with N·m unit ✓; (d) captions distinguishing large-d (topples) from small-d (stable) ✓. A model with a force arrow but NO distance annotation indicates the student can name the formula but has not yet connected 'distance from the pivot' to the physical geometry of the stool — this student needs a targeted question at the start of Lesson 3: 'Point to the pivot on your stool diagram. Now point to the distance d. Are they the same point?'

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, reflect on the following questions: (1) FORMULA VS. CONCEPT: Did students calculate M = F × d correctly while still confusing which point is the pivot? If yes, design a 3-minute 'pivot identification' retrieval activity to open Lesson 3. (2) PHENOMENON CONNECTION: When students annotated the Jiko stool model, did they instinctively measure 'd' from the jerrycan to the nearest leg (correct pivot), or to the centre of the stool (common error)? The correct pivot is the leg that acts as the fulcrum during tipping — if students used the centre, address this explicitly at the start of Lesson 3. (3) DQB QUALITY: Count the number of 'I Wonder' questions that ask about TWO forces or OPPOSITE directions. If more than half the class generated such questions, you can introduce the Principle of Moments in Lesson 3 with confidence that students are cognitively ready. If fewer than a quarter did, begin Lesson 3 with a 5-minute re-engagement using the metre rule: 'What if I put a mass on BOTH sides of the pivot — can I make it balance? Try it.' (4) ENGAGEMENT: Did the mkokoteni and school-gate examples resonate as genuinely familiar? If students seemed unfamiliar with the mkokoteni, substitute a wheelbarrow used on a Kenyan farm, or a water pump handle at a borehole in the next lesson's examples. (5) PACING: If Phase 3 (Explain) ran over time, consider pre-printing the worked examples rather than dictating them, so instructional time focuses on the reasoning steps rather than copying. Note which groups completed the contrasting mini-diagram in Phase 5 — groups that did not reach it should be given it as a 5-minute starter task in Lesson 3.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Students observed that moving a 500 g mass further from the pencil-pivot on a metre rule increased the tipping effect, even though the mass (and therefore the force) did not change. They also observed, in a teacher demonstration, that pushing a door near the hinge requires far more effort than pushing near the outer edge. In the anchoring phenomenon, they re-observed that placing the jerrycan at the corner of the Jiko stool (far from the supporting legs) produced a dramatic toppling effect that placing it near the centre did not.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Students learned that the turning effect of a force is called its MOMENT, and it is calculated using the formula M = F × d, where F is the applied force in Newtons and d is the perpendicular distance from the force's line of action to the pivot, in metres. The SI unit of moment is the Newton-metre (N·m). A larger force OR a larger perpendicular distance produces a larger moment. This explains why the jerrycan at the corner of the Jiko stool produces a larger toppling moment than the same jerrycan placed at the centre.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: Students can now partially explain the anchoring phenomenon: the Jiko stool topples when the jerrycan is at the corner because the perpendicular distance from the jerrycan's weight (force) to the nearest supporting leg (pivot) is large, producing a large moment (turning effect). When the jerrycan is near the centre, the distance d is small, so the moment is small and the stool remains upright. What students cannot yet explain: what happens when there are turning forces acting in OPPOSITE directions (clockwise and anticlockwise) — this is the Principle of Moments, which is the focus of Lesson 3.

LESSONS.aresKeywords: moment of a force, turning effect, pivot, fulcrum, perpendicular distance, M = F × d, Newton-metre, clockwise, anticlockwise, Jiko stool, toppling, stability, mkokoteni, school gate, force arrow, moment calculation, Driving Question Board, cumulative model, evidence statement

LESSONS.title: Lesson 3: Centre of Gravity and the Tipping Point — Why Does the Jiko Stool Fall?

slo.purpose: To investigate how the position of the centre of gravity relative to the base of support determines whether an object is stable, unstable, or in neutral equilibrium.
slo.knowledge: By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) define centre of gravity as the point through which the total weight of a body appears to act; (b) explain stable, unstable, and neutral equilibrium using centre of gravity and base of support; (c) state the conditions under which a body topples — that is, when the vertical line through the centre of gravity falls outside the base of support.
slo.skills: By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) locate the centre of gravity of irregular flat objects using a plumb-line method; (b) design and carry out an investigation to compare the stability of objects with different base widths and heights; (c) construct evidence-based arguments linking centre of gravity height and base width to toppling behaviour observed in the Jiko stool phenomenon.
slo.attitudes: The learner appreciates that physical principles governing balance and stability are embedded in everyday Kenyan objects — from Jiko stools to water-carrying jerrycans — and values the role of careful observation and measurement in building scientific explanations.
slo.keyInquiry: How do forces determine the stability and equilibrium of objects we encounter every day?
slo.purposeInStoryline: In Lesson 2, learners used the principle of moments (sum of clockwise moments = sum of anticlockwise moments) to explain why a see-saw balances at a particular pivot position. Lesson 3 now advances the storyline by asking: given that turning forces matter, WHERE exactly does the total weight of the Jiko stool — and its load — act? Learners discover the centre of gravity concept, investigate how its height and the width of the base of support together determine the toppling condition, and build a precise geometric explanation for why the jerrycan-loaded stool falls. This sets up Lesson 4, which will quantify the restoring and toppling moments to predict the exact tipping angle.
slo.safetyNotes: Ensure all stool/block models and jerrycan props are placed on the floor or a stable bench — never on a desk edge. Students must not stand on stools during demonstrations. When suspending objects from a plumb line, ensure the string is securely tied and scissors are handled safely. Floors must be clear of bags and water to prevent slipping if an object falls. Teacher supervises all group tilting activities and reminds learners to tilt objects slowly and in a controlled manner.

LESSONS.overview: Learners begin by revisiting the anchoring phenomenon — the toppling Jiko stool — and the DQB from Lessons 1 and 2. The teacher introduces a new sub-question: 'But WHERE does the weight of the stool actually pull from?' Learners first predict where the centre of a flat cardboard shape 'balances', then discover the centre of gravity experimentally using a plumb-line method with irregular cardboard cut-outs shaped like a Jiko stool profile and a sufuria lid. Groups then investigate how raising the centre of gravity (stacking blocks or books) and narrowing the base affects when a model 'topples'. The Explain phase formalises the three states of equilibrium with diagrams and connects them directly to the stool-and-jerrycan scenario. The DQB is updated, and learners revise their cumulative explanatory models with annotated diagrams showing the centre of gravity, base of support, and the critical tipping line.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners are shown a cardboard silhouette cut in the profile of a Jiko stool (wide base, narrow top) and an irregularly shaped piece of cardboard resembling a sufuria lid. Working individually, each learner marks with a pencil dot where they think the object's weight is 'centred' — the single point from which the whole weight seems to pull downward. Learners then share their predictions with a shoulder partner, noting agreements and disagreements. The teacher collects predictions on the board using a quick show-of-hands heat map (5 zones marked on a projected image of the silhouette). Learners also write a one-sentence prediction: 'I think the stool topples because …', connecting to their prior knowledge from Lesson 2 on moments.
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher displays the two cardboard cut-outs (Jiko stool profile and sufuria-lid shape) using a document camera or by holding them up clearly. Teacher says: 'Before we touch any equipment, I want you to mark — just with a mental dot — where you think the entire weight of this shape is trying to pull downward from. You have 30 seconds to decide silently.' [WAIT 30 seconds — no talking.] Teacher then says: 'Turn to your shoulder partner. You have 60 seconds — tell each other WHERE you marked and WHY you placed it there.' [WAIT 60 seconds.] Teacher cold-calls 4 learners (one from each corner of the room): 'Amina — where did you mark it and what was your reasoning?' Repeats for Ochieng, Wanjiku, and Mwangi. Teacher records the four prediction zones on the board without affirming or correcting. Teacher then asks the class: 'We used the PRINCIPLE OF MOMENTS in Lesson 2. How might that idea help us find this balance point today?' [WAIT 10 seconds.] Cold-calls 2 learners. Teacher closes the Predict phase: 'Hold your prediction — we are about to find out who is right, and more importantly, WHY it matters for our toppling stool.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Strategy: Prediction Mapping. By committing a prediction to paper and to a partner before any instruction, learners activate prior knowledge (Lesson 2 moments) and generate cognitive dissonance when their predictions inevitably scatter across different zones. This productive uncertainty primes learners to notice and explain the evidence they are about to gather, rather than passively receiving the answer.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Teacher scans individual prediction dots and listens to partner-talk. Look for whether learners place the dot near the geometric centre vs. near the heavy base — a common misconception is that it must be at the exact geometric centre. If more than half the class places the dot at the geometric centre of the irregular shape (ignoring mass distribution), teacher notes this as a target misconception to address explicitly in the Explain phase.

framework.learnerExperience: ACTIVITY A — Plumb-Line Centre of Gravity Hunt (12 min): Each group of 4 receives: one irregular cardboard cut-out (Jiko stool profile), one sufuria-lid shape, a pin-board or corkboard, a length of string with a small nut/bolt as a plumb bob, a ruler, and a pencil. Learners suspend each shape from three different pin-holes along its edge, each time hanging the plumb-line string from the same pin and drawing the vertical line it traces across the cardboard. The intersection of the three lines is labelled 'CG'. Groups compare their CG mark to their original prediction dot. ACTIVITY B — Tipping Towers (8 min): Each group receives a stack of 4 wooden blocks (or thick textbooks). Groups build two towers: Tower A = wide base (4 blocks side by side, 1 block high) and Tower B = narrow base (1 block wide, 4 blocks tall). Groups place a small 500 g mass (a bag of maize or a water bottle) first on the centre, then gradually shift it to the edge, observing at what point each tower topples. Groups record: (i) which tower toppled first, (ii) how far the mass had moved when toppling occurred, (iii) a sketch showing the tower, mass, and a dotted vertical line from the estimated CG.
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher circulates during Activity A. At the 4-minute mark, teacher pauses groups: 'Freeze. Hold up your first plumb line. What direction is the string pulling?' [WAIT 10 seconds.] 'That direction IS the direction gravity is pulling. Draw that line NOW.' Teacher models on one group's board if needed. At the 8-minute mark, teacher asks the whole class (raised-voice whole-class prompt): 'Before you move to Activity B — where did your three lines meet? Is it inside the shape or outside? Thumbs up if inside, thumbs sideways if on the edge, thumbs down if outside.' Teacher notes the distribution. During Activity B, teacher circulates and asks targeted groups: 'Ochieng's group — when the mass was at the corner and the tower fell, describe exactly what happened to that vertical CG line just before it fell.' [WAIT 10 seconds.] Cold-calls 3 groups to share their tipping observations. Teacher does NOT give the explanation yet — instead says: 'Make sure your sketch captures EXACTLY where the CG was when it tipped. That sketch is your evidence.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Strategy: Evidence Sketching with Annotated Diagrams. Requiring learners to draw the vertical CG line on their tipping tower sketches forces them to spatially relate the abstract concept of centre of gravity to the observable physical event of toppling. This embodied, visual record becomes the primary evidence artefact they will use in the Explain phase to construct a causal argument.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: In Activity A, all groups should find that their three plumb lines converge at a single point — if they do not, teacher checks that the shape was truly allowed to hang freely and the line was drawn while hanging (not after removal). In Activity B, the key observable indicator is whether learner sketches show the CG vertical line moving beyond the base edge at the tipping point — if sketches do not show this, teacher prompts: 'Draw a straight vertical line downward from the CG mark. Does it land inside or outside the base?'

framework.learnerExperience: Groups share their Activity B sketches via a Gallery Walk (each group posts their sketch on the wall; learners circulate for 4 minutes with sticky notes, writing one observation per sketch). The class then reconvenes. Using their evidence, learners co-construct definitions of the three states of equilibrium — stable, unstable, and neutral — guided by teacher questioning. Learners draw three canonical diagrams in their notebooks: (1) a wide-based cone — CG low, vertical line falls inside base after tilt → returns; (2) a narrow tall block — CG high, vertical line falls outside base after small tilt → topples; (3) a sphere on a flat surface — CG always above contact point → rolls without toppling. Learners then apply the model directly to the anchoring phenomenon: they annotate a printed diagram of the Jiko stool (one with the jerrycan at the centre, one with it at the corner) showing: CG position, base of support, and the vertical CG line, then write a two-sentence causal explanation of why the stool topples in one case and not the other.
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher opens the Gallery Walk debrief: 'I saw some fascinating sketches. Wanjiku's group — read out the sticky note someone left on your sketch.' After 2 responses, teacher says: 'Let's build our shared explanation together. Look at your Activity B sketches. In EVERY case where the tower fell — where was the vertical CG line?' [WAIT 12 seconds.] Cold-calls 3 learners. Teacher writes on the board: 'A body topples when the vertical line through its centre of gravity passes _______ the base of support.' Teacher waits — does NOT fill in the blank. [WAIT 10 seconds.] Cold-calls learner: 'Mwangi — finish that sentence.' Teacher formalises the vocabulary: 'Centre of gravity', 'base of support', 'stable equilibrium', 'unstable equilibrium', 'neutral equilibrium' — writing each term as learners define them from evidence, not the other way around. Teacher then directs: 'Now apply this to OUR stool. On the diagram in front of you — draw the CG for the jerrycan-at-corner case. Draw the vertical CG line. Does it fall inside or outside the three-legged base triangle? That is your answer to why the stool topples.' Teacher circulates for 3 minutes while learners annotate. Teacher cold-calls one group to explain their diagram to the class. Teacher closes: 'This is the physics our Kenyan jua kali craftspeople use — even if they have never written it down — when they decide how wide to make a stool's legs.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Strategy: Co-constructed Definition from Evidence (Claim-Evidence-Reasoning). Rather than presenting definitions first, the teacher uses the Gallery Walk and targeted questioning to pull the definition out of learners' own evidence sketches. This ensures that the formal vocabulary of 'centre of gravity' and 'base of support' is anchored to physical observations, making the concepts retrievable and transferable rather than memorised labels.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Learner annotations of the Jiko stool diagram must show (a) the CG marked at a plausible location above the stool seat (higher when jerrycan is on top), (b) a vertical line drawn downward from CG, and (c) a written sentence stating whether the line falls inside or outside the base triangle. Teacher collects 6 annotated diagrams (one per group) as exit evidence. If learners cannot place the CG correctly — for example, placing it at the jerrycan itself rather than the combined system — teacher re-prompts: 'The jerrycan and the stool are ONE system now. Where is the combined weight acting?'

framework.learnerExperience: The class reviews the DQB constructed in Lessons 1 and 2. Each group selects two sticky notes from the DQB that they believe Lesson 3 has now given them evidence to answer or partially answer. Groups write a revised answer card (green sticky note) and place it beside the original question (yellow sticky note). The class also generates at least two NEW questions provoked by today's lesson — for example: 'Can we calculate EXACTLY how far we can move the jerrycan before the stool tips?' or 'Does the shape of the base (triangle vs. square) change how stable the stool is?' — and adds these to the DQB as orange sticky notes. The teacher records any questions that directly anticipate Lesson 4 content (calculating toppling moments) and circles them as 'next lesson targets'.
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher points to the DQB: 'We started this unit with big questions about the toppling stool. Let's see what we can NOW answer with today's evidence.' Teacher reads aloud two questions from the DQB: 'Why does the stool topple when the jerrycan is at the corner?' and 'Does the height of the load matter?' Teacher says: 'Discuss with your group: can we answer either of these NOW? Use your diagrams as evidence. 90 seconds.' [WAIT 90 seconds.] Groups post their green answer cards. Teacher cold-calls Amina's group: 'Read your answer card. What evidence from today supports it?' After the group responds, teacher probes: 'What part of this question do we still NOT fully understand?' [WAIT 10 seconds.] Teacher writes any new questions on the DQB and says: 'These orange questions are what will drive our next lessons. Notice that we now have the GEOMETRY of toppling — but do we have the NUMBERS? Can we PREDICT the tipping angle? That is our next challenge.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Strategy: Driving Question Board (DQB) Revision with Claim Cards. The DQB serves as a public, cumulative record of the class's growing understanding. By physically replacing question cards with evidence-based answer cards, learners experience epistemic progress — they can see what they have explained and what remains open. The new orange questions demonstrate that good scientific explanations generate new questions, mirroring authentic scientific practice.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Teacher checks that green answer cards reference specific evidence from today's activities (e.g., 'When we moved the mass to the corner, the CG line moved outside the base — that is why the stool topples') rather than restating the question or giving vague answers. Cards that only say 'because of centre of gravity' without referencing the base of support indicate incomplete understanding and flag those groups for re-teaching in Lesson 4.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners return to the cumulative explanatory model they began in Lesson 1 (a labelled diagram of the Jiko stool phenomenon) and revised in Lesson 2 (adding moment arrows). Today, they add a third layer: (a) a dot labelled 'CG' on both the stool-alone and the stool-plus-jerrycan versions; (b) a vertical dashed arrow from the CG pointing downward labelled 'Weight acts here'; (c) the triangular or rectangular outline of the base of support labelled 'Base of Support'; (d) a critical tipping line — a dashed vertical line from CG — coloured red if it falls outside the base and green if inside; and (e) a written caption of no more than three sentences explaining, in the learner's own words, why the stool topples when the jerrycan is at the corner. Groups share their revised models briefly (each group holds up their model; teacher does a rapid visual scan). Teacher asks two targeted questions to the whole class before closing.
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher distributes or directs learners to open their Lesson 1 model diagrams. Teacher says: 'Your model has been growing since Lesson 1. Today it gets smarter. I need you to add EXACTLY these four things —' [writes the four additions on the board, numbered]. 'You have 10 minutes. Go.' [WAIT — teacher circulates actively, checking that CG is placed on the combined system, not just the stool alone.] At the 8-minute mark, teacher says: 'Freeze. I am going to ask two questions. Think before you answer.' Question 1: 'If a craftsman in Kericho builds a stool that is taller but narrows the legs — what happens to stability? Tell your neighbour in one sentence.' [WAIT 15 seconds.] Cold-calls Ochieng. Question 2: 'A mama mboga in Gikomba market carries a heavy debe of tomatoes on her head. Why does she walk carefully and keep her back straight?' [WAIT 15 seconds.] Cold-calls Wanjiku. Teacher connects both answers back to the model: 'The physics is IDENTICAL — centre of gravity, base of support, and the tipping line. Your model now explains REAL LIFE.' Teacher closes: 'In Lesson 4, we will use moments calculations to find the EXACT tipping angle. Keep this model — you will need it.'
