framework.sensemakingStrategy: Strategy: Cumulative Annotated Model Revision. The model-building phase is not a separate activity — it IS the sensemaking product. By requiring learners to add a new conceptual layer (CG, base of support, tipping line) to the same diagram they have been developing since Lesson 1, the model becomes a visible record of conceptual growth. The mama mboga analogy grounds abstract physics in a vivid Kenyan cultural context, reinforcing transfer.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Teacher's rapid visual scan during the hold-up checks for three non-negotiables: (1) CG dot is on the combined stool-plus-jerrycan system, not at the jerrycan alone; (2) the tipping line (vertical from CG) is drawn and correctly colour-coded red/green based on position relative to the base; (3) the three-sentence caption uses the words 'centre of gravity', 'base of support', and 'topples'. Models missing any of the three are flagged for the teacher to address at the start of Lesson 4 with a quick targeted re-teach.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, reflect on the following questions: (1) Did learners successfully transfer the plumb-line CG finding to the stool diagram, or did a significant number still place the CG at the geometric centre regardless of mass distribution? If so, plan a brief 5-minute re-teach at the start of Lesson 4 using a lollipop stick with a lump of ugali clay on one end to make mass distribution visceral. (2) During the Gallery Walk, were learners' sticky-note comments surface-level ('good diagram') or evidence-based ('the CG line goes outside the base here')? If surface-level, introduce a sentence starter scaffold next time: 'This diagram shows _______ because _______.' (3) Did the mama mboga and Kericho craftsman examples resonate? Note which analogies generated the most engagement — these are your cultural anchors for Lesson 4. (4) Review the 6 collected annotated Jiko stool diagrams before Lesson 4: tally how many correctly show CG on the combined system. If fewer than 4 of 6 are correct, open Lesson 4 with a whole-class error analysis of an anonymised incorrect diagram before proceeding to tipping angle calculations.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Students observed that a plumb-line hanging from different points on an irregular cardboard shape always intersects at the same point (the centre of gravity), and that a tall narrow block tipped when a mass was moved to its edge while a wide low block did not — directly mirroring the jerrycan-on-stool behaviour from the anchoring phenomenon.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Students learned that every object has a centre of gravity — the single point through which its total weight appears to act — and that a body topples when the vertical line through its centre of gravity passes outside its base of support; they also distinguished between stable, unstable, and neutral equilibrium using this principle.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: Students explained why the Jiko stool topples when the jerrycan is placed at the corner: the jerrycan raises and shifts the combined centre of gravity of the stool-plus-load system until the vertical line through the CG falls outside the triangular base of support formed by the three legs, generating an unbalanced toppling moment with no restoring moment to counteract it.

LESSONS.aresKeywords: centre of gravity, base of support, stable equilibrium, unstable equilibrium, neutral equilibrium, toppling condition, plumb-line method, moments, Jiko stool, tipping point, weight distribution, restoring moment, toppling moment

LESSONS.title: The Principle of Moments: Balancing Forces on a Bao Plank

slo.purpose: To investigate the Principle of Moments experimentally and apply it to solve quantitative equilibrium problems in Kenyan everyday contexts.
slo.knowledge: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to: (a) define the moment of a force and state its SI unit (Newton-metre); (b) state the Principle of Moments; (c) calculate clockwise and anti-clockwise moments in given scenarios; (d) apply the Principle of Moments to solve quantitative problems involving balanced beams.
slo.skills: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to: (a) plan and conduct a fair experiment using improvised materials (metre rule, pencil pivot, bags of beans, coins) to gather moment data; (b) tabulate and analyse clockwise and anti-clockwise moment values; (c) identify patterns in data and generalise them into the Principle of Moments; (d) use the principle to solve structured problems including a traditional Kenyan see-saw scenario.
slo.attitudes: By the end of this lesson, learners will: (a) appreciate that systematic data collection leads to reliable scientific principles; (b) demonstrate responsibility by handling shared lab materials carefully; (c) show curiosity in connecting mathematical patterns to the real-world behaviour of the Jiko stool and other balanced objects.
slo.keyInquiry: How do forces determine the stability and equilibrium of objects we encounter every day?
slo.purposeInStoryline: In Lessons 1–3 learners identified turning forces (moments), located the centre of gravity of irregular objects, and connected the position of the centre of gravity to toppling. This lesson is the quantitative heart of the sequence: learners measure moments directly, discover that balance requires clockwise moments to equal anti-clockwise moments, and use this principle to predict whether a load placement on the Jiko stool will cause toppling — adding precise, calculable meaning to the phenomenon for the first time.
slo.safetyNotes: Ensure the metre rule is stable on the pencil pivot before releasing masses. Bags of beans should be tied securely to prevent spillage. Coins should not be placed near the edge of desks to avoid falling. Learners must not stand on stools or furniture during any demonstration. Remind learners to keep the workspace tidy to prevent trip hazards.

LESSONS.overview: Learners begin by reviewing the Driving Question Board (DQB) to identify what is still unexplained about the Jiko stool phenomenon — specifically, WHY position of the load matters quantitatively. Working in groups of four, they set up a metre-rule balance on a pencil pivot (representing the log fulcrum of a Kenyan bao see-saw plank) and systematically vary the mass and distance on each side, recording clockwise and anti-clockwise moments in a structured table. After pooling class data, learners spot the pattern (clockwise moments = anti-clockwise moments at balance) and formally state the Principle of Moments. The teacher then bridges back to the Jiko stool: learners calculate the moment of the jerrycan at different positions and determine the threshold at which toppling occurs. The lesson closes with model revision — groups annotate their cumulative diagrams with moment arms, force arrows, and the balance-condition label — and the DQB is updated to mark newly answered sub-questions.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners individually examine a labelled diagram of a bao plank (a 1-metre wooden plank balanced on a log fulcrum at its midpoint) with two Kenyan children sitting at different positions. Child A (mass 40 kg) sits 30 cm from the centre; Child B (mass 20 kg) sits on the opposite side at an unknown distance. Learners write individual predictions: (1) Will the plank be balanced, tip to A's side, or tip to B's side? (2) Where must Child B sit for balance? (3) What rule or pattern might explain your prediction? Learners then share predictions with a partner (Think-Pair) before four predictions are cold-called to the board — teacher records them without evaluation under the heading 'Our Predictions'.
framework.teacherMoves: Display the bao plank diagram on the board or printed handout. Say: 'Before we do any measuring today, I want your brain to take a risk. Look at this diagram of Amina and Juma on a bao plank. Predict: will it balance? Where must Juma sit? Write your thinking — numbers welcome, even if you are guessing.' Allow 3 minutes of silent individual writing. After Think-Pair (2 minutes), cold-call exactly 4 students: 'Fatuma — what did you predict and why?', 'Otieno — do you agree or disagree with Fatuma? What is different about your reasoning?' Record predictions verbatim. Say: 'Hold your predictions. By the end of this lesson you will be able to prove mathematically who is right.' DO NOT validate or correct predictions yet. Wait time: 10–12 seconds after each cold-call before prompting.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Think-Pair-Share with Prediction Anchoring — learners activate prior intuition about balance before data collection. Recording predictions publicly creates cognitive commitment and a reference point for the 'explain' phase, making the moment of insight more memorable.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher scans written predictions and listens to pair talk. Observable indicators: (a) At least 60% of learners attempt a numerical estimate for Child B's distance, showing they intuit that both mass and distance matter. (b) Listen for the word 'distance' or 'farther' appearing in reasoning — this signals readiness to formalise. Red flag: if most learners predict balance based on mass alone (ignoring distance), teacher notes this misconception to address explicitly in Phase 3.

framework.learnerExperience: Groups of four set up the metre-rule balance experiment: a 30-cm pencil taped to the desk acts as the fulcrum; the metre rule rests on it at the 50 cm mark. Groups hang small bags of dry beans (labelled in grams using a kitchen scale or known coin masses) at measured distances from the pivot on both sides, adjusting until the rule is perfectly horizontal. Each group completes at least 6 trials, varying mass and distance independently. They record: (1) Mass left (g), Distance left from pivot (cm), Clockwise Moment = F × d (N·cm); (2) Mass right (g), Distance right from pivot (cm), Anti-clockwise Moment = F × d (N·cm); (3) Status: Balanced / Tips Left / Tips Right. Groups also test ONE deliberately unbalanced case and note what happens. Data is recorded in a shared class table projected or drawn on the board. Midway, the teacher pauses all groups for a 2-minute 'Data Checkpoint' to verify units and calculation method before groups continue.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute printed data table templates. Demonstrate the setup with one group in front of the class (60 seconds max): 'I am placing a 100 g bean bag at 20 cm on the left. Watch — the rule tips left. Now I move a 50 g bag on the right to 40 cm. Watch what happens.' Ask: 'What did you notice? What two things did I change?' (Wait 10 seconds.) Say: 'Your job is to find the mathematical pattern hiding in the numbers. Every group must complete at least 6 trials. Record EVERY result — even the messy ones.' Circulate every 3–4 minutes. At the Data Checkpoint (15 minutes in), say: 'Pause. Show me your moment column. If you multiplied mass in grams by distance in centimetres — good. Now: what do you notice when the rule is balanced? Talk to your group for 90 seconds.' Cold-call 2 groups to share emerging patterns. Prompt if needed: 'Look at the left moment and the right moment in your balanced trials — what relationship do you see?' Wait 12 seconds.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Structured Data Table with Guided Pattern Spotting — the table design forces learners to compute both moments side-by-side, making the equality relationship visually obvious. The mid-activity checkpoint interrupts procedural work to prompt relational thinking, mirroring how scientists pause to interpret during data collection.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (a) All groups can correctly compute F × d for at least 4 balanced trials. (b) At the Data Checkpoint, at least 2 groups spontaneously state 'the two moments are equal' or 'both sides give the same number' — this is the target insight. (c) Monitor for unit confusion (mixing cm and m, or grams and Newtons) — if seen in more than 2 groups, pause the class for a 90-second unit clarification. Red flag: a group that always gets 'balanced' by moving the rule rather than adjusting masses/distances is not controlling variables — redirect immediately.

framework.learnerExperience: The class pools data into the board table. Learners examine all balanced trials and, guided by teacher questioning, articulate the pattern in their own words before the teacher offers the formal statement. Learners then copy and annotate the Principle of Moments in their notebooks with a diagram. Next, they solve three structured problems individually: (1) The bao plank problem from Phase 1 — now solved mathematically, predictions are checked. (2) A sufuria of ugali (mass 3 kg) is placed 0.4 m from the pivot of a market weighing plank; what mass of maize grain placed 0.6 m on the other side will balance it? (3) A Jiko stool leg acts as a pivot: if a 20-litre jerrycan (20 kg) is placed 0.25 m from the central pivot point, what restoring moment would a 5 kg counterweight at 0.5 m provide — and is it sufficient to prevent toppling? Learners compare their Phase 1 predictions to their calculated answers and note what they got right/wrong and why.
framework.teacherMoves: Point to the class data table. Say: 'Look at every row where the rule was balanced. Stare at the two moment columns. What is always true?' Wait 12 seconds. Cold-call 3 students. After a learner states the equality, say: 'Say that again in one sentence as if you are writing a law of physics.' Write the student's version on the board. Then say: 'Here is how physicists state it formally:' — write verbatim: 'For a body in equilibrium, the sum of clockwise moments about any point equals the sum of anti-clockwise moments about the same point.' Say: 'Underline the word ANY — this is powerful. It does not matter which point you choose.' For problem 3 (Jiko stool), explicitly bridge: 'We are back to our stool. The jerrycan is not on a see-saw — but we can TREAT the tipping edge as the pivot. Let us calculate.' Pause after each problem for 2 minutes of silent individual work before reviewing. Do not solve for learners — ask: 'What is your pivot? What are your two moments? Which is larger?' Cold-call one learner per problem step.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Pattern → Principle → Application (PPA) Sequence — learners move from raw data pattern to formal scientific principle to quantitative application in one lesson. Explicitly returning to the Jiko stool phenomenon in problem 3 closes the predict-observe-explain loop and demonstrates the explanatory power of the Principle of Moments. Prediction checking in Phase 1 creates a personal 'aha moment' for each learner.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (a) In their notebooks, learners write the Principle of Moments correctly without copying from board within 2 minutes (cover the board briefly to check). (b) At least 75% of learners correctly solve the sufuria-of-ugali problem independently (correct pivot choice, correct F × d calculation, correct unknown mass). (c) In problem 3, listen for learners correctly identifying the tipping edge as the pivot — this transfers learning to the phenomenon. Red flag: learners who recalculate using weight (in Newtons) rather than mass — affirm this as more precise and note it as a strength, not an error.

framework.learnerExperience: The DQB is displayed. Groups are given 3 minutes to re-read all posted sub-questions from Lessons 1–3. Each group nominates one sub-question that today's experiment has now answered and writes a one-sentence evidence-based answer on a green sticky note. They also add at least one new sub-question that today's lesson has raised (e.g., 'Does the shape of the base change the pivot point for toppling?' or 'What happens to moments when more than two forces act?') on a yellow sticky note. The teacher facilitates a 4-minute whole-class review: 3 groups share their green notes; 2 new yellow questions are read aloud and posted.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Our DQB has been growing since Lesson 1. Today we have a powerful new tool — the Principle of Moments. Let us audit our board.' Point to specific sub-questions: 'Group C — does today's experiment answer the question about WHY position of the jerrycan matters? Write your answer on green.' After groups write, cold-call 3 groups to read their green notes. For each, ask the class: 'Do we agree this question is now answered? Thumbs up or thumbs sideways.' For yellow new questions, say: 'Good science always opens new doors. What do you still wonder about?' After 2 new questions are posted, say: 'We will chase these in Lessons 5 and 6.' Update the board visually — move answered questions to a 'We Now Know' column.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: DQB Audit with Colour-Coded Progress Tracking — green/yellow sticky note protocol makes knowledge growth visible and motivates learners by showing that their questions are driving the unit. Moving cards to 'We Now Know' provides a tangible sense of epistemic progress and reinforces metacognitive awareness of what counts as an answer.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (a) Each group correctly identifies at least one genuinely answered sub-question (not a trivially obvious one) — this shows integration of lesson content with prior questions. (b) New yellow questions are conceptually relevant to moments or stability (not off-topic) — this shows productive disciplinary curiosity. (c) Class thumbs-up agreement on green notes is above 70% — if lower, the teacher notes the disputed answer for follow-up.

framework.learnerExperience: Each group retrieves their cumulative model diagram (built across Lessons 1–3, showing the Jiko stool, centre of gravity, tipping edge, and weight arrow). They now add: (1) a labelled moment arm (d) from the tipping-edge pivot to the line of action of the jerrycan's weight; (2) a labelled moment arm for any stabilising force (e.g., the stool's own weight acting through the centre of gravity); (3) a 'balance condition' annotation: 'Stool stable IF: anti-clockwise moment > clockwise moment about tipping edge'; (4) a colour-coded moment arrow (clockwise = red, anti-clockwise = blue). Groups display models and do a 90-second gallery walk to view two other groups' diagrams before returning to make one revision based on what they noticed.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute model diagrams and coloured pens. Say: 'Your model has shown us WHERE the centre of gravity is and WHAT a tipping edge is. Today we can make it speak mathematics. Add the moment arms — the perpendicular distance from your pivot to the line of action of each force. Label them d₁ and d₂.' Circulate, checking that moment arms are drawn perpendicular to the force arrows (common error: drawing along the beam rather than perpendicular). Correct gently: 'Is that arm perpendicular to the weight arrow? Check with a set square or the corner of your book.' After gallery walk, say: 'You had 90 seconds to be a critic. What is one thing you will improve in your own model?' Cold-call 2 learners. Close by saying: 'Next lesson we examine what happens when multiple forces act — and we will finally be able to fully explain why the wide stool never topples.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Model Annotation with Gallery Walk Critique — annotating the shared model embeds the Principle of Moments into the ongoing explanatory framework for the phenomenon, preventing it from being treated as an isolated formula. The gallery walk introduces peer critique as a scientific practice (analogous to peer review), encouraging learners to refine representations.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (a) Moment arms are drawn correctly (perpendicular to the force arrow, from the pivot) in at least 3 of 4 groups — teacher checks during circulation. (b) The balance condition annotation uses an inequality (anti-clockwise moment vs. clockwise moment), not just an equals sign — this shows understanding that toppling occurs when equality is broken. (c) After the gallery walk, at least 60% of groups make a substantive revision (not just cosmetic) — this shows genuine engagement with peer critique.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, reflect on the following questions: (1) Did learners successfully spot the moment equality pattern from their own data before I stated the Principle — or did I give it away too early? If the latter, how can I extend the Data Checkpoint next time to give groups more time to articulate the pattern themselves? (2) Which of the three quantitative problems caused the most difficulty — was it choosing the correct pivot, converting units, or setting up the equation? Plan a targeted 5-minute opener for Lesson 5 to address the most common error. (3) Were learners able to transfer the Principle back to the Jiko stool context in problem 3, or did they treat it as an abstract formula? If transfer was weak, consider adding a physical demonstration with the actual stool in Lesson 5. (4) How well did the DQB audit go — did groups identify genuinely answered questions, or surface-level ones? Review the green sticky notes after class and plan responses to the yellow questions to ensure Lessons 5 and 6 address learner-generated curiosity. (5) Were moment arms drawn correctly on the cumulative model? If perpendicularity was a widespread error, prepare a short visual correction at the start of Lesson 5 before model revision continues.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Learners balanced a metre-rule on a pencil pivot using bags of beans and coins, recording mass and distance data across 6+ trials and noting whether the rule was balanced or tipping.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: When a ruler (or any beam) is balanced, the clockwise moment (force × distance) always equals the anti-clockwise moment about the pivot — this is the Principle of Moments. The position of a load relative to the pivot determines whether a turning effect causes toppling or stability.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: The Jiko stool topples when the jerrycan is placed far from the centre because its clockwise moment about the tipping edge exceeds the anti-clockwise moment of the stool's own weight acting through the centre of gravity. The wider stool has a larger anti-clockwise stabilising moment, so the same jerrycan load does not cause toppling — the moments remain balanced.

LESSONS.aresKeywords: Principle of Moments, clockwise moment, anti-clockwise moment, moment arm, pivot, equilibrium, turning force, Newton-metre, beam balance, centre of gravity, tipping edge, Jiko stool, bao plank, sufuria, jerrycan, stability, torque, perpendicular distance, balanced beam, force × distance

LESSONS.title: How Do Engineers Keep Things From Falling? — Factors Affecting Stability and Real-Life Applications
LESSONS.aresKeywords: centre of gravity, base of support, stability, toppling, moment, perpendicular distance, engineering design, equilibrium

slo.purpose: Students investigate how lowering the centre of gravity and widening the base of support independently increase the stability of an object, then apply these principles to analyse real engineering contexts including crane design, double-decker buses, and a Nairobi construction-site case study.
slo.knowledge: Students will be able to: (1) State that stability increases when the centre of gravity is lowered and when the base of support is widened; (2) Explain that an object topples when the vertical line through its centre of gravity falls outside its base of support; (3) Identify engineering strategies that exploit these principles in structures and vehicles.
slo.skills: Students will be able to: (1) Conduct a fair-test investigation manipulating one variable at a time (height of centre of gravity, width of base) and recording tipping angles; (2) Analyse a case-study document to extract evidence of moment and stability principles; (3) Update a cumulative explanatory model with new causal labels and engineering examples.
slo.attitudes: Students appreciate that physics principles underlie everyday engineering decisions that protect human lives, and they develop curiosity about careers in civil, mechanical, and transport engineering.
slo.keyInquiry: How do forces determine the stability and equilibrium of objects we encounter every day?
slo.purposeInStoryline: Lessons 1 through 4 established WHAT toppling is and HOW to classify equilibrium states. Lesson 5 advances the storyline by answering WHY some designs resist toppling and HOW engineers deliberately manipulate centre of gravity and base width to keep structures and vehicles safe — directly resolving the core puzzle of the Toppling Jiko Stool phenomenon and equipping students to write a full scientific explanation in Lesson 6.
slo.safetyNotes: Ensure clay lumps are secured to boxes before tilting to prevent unexpected falling objects. Students should keep feet clear of the table edge during tipping tests. The Nairobi crane case-study card contains accident imagery; preview with sensitivity and remind students the focus is engineering analysis, not graphic content.

LESSONS.overview: In a 40-minute lesson students run two back-to-back mini-investigations using tall cardboard boxes, clay, and adjustable cardboard flaps to isolate the effect of (a) lowering the centre of gravity and (b) widening the base of support on stability. They record critical tipping angles and construct causal explanations. A structured case-study card on a documented Nairobi construction-site crane incident is then analysed in pairs using the moment and stability vocabulary built across the unit. The cumulative class model — a labelled diagram of the Jiko stool — is updated with stability-factor labels and an engineering inset sketch. The lesson ends with a DQB review sticky-note that advances the driving question from classification to prediction and design.

framework.learnerExperience: Students are shown two identical tall cardboard boxes (roughly 30 cm tall, 10 cm square base). Box A has a palm-sized lump of clay stuck near its top; Box B has the same clay lump stuck to its inside base. The teacher asks: 'Without touching them, predict which box will topple first when I tilt the table surface slowly — and write down the TWO things about the box you think control that.' Students write individual predictions in their exercise books (2 minutes), then share with a neighbour (think-pair, 1 minute). A quick show-of-hands poll is taken: hands up if you predict Box A falls first. The teacher records the class split on the board without revealing the answer. Students also briefly revisit their Lesson 4 cumulative model sketch and annotate it with a question mark next to any factor they feel is still unexplained.
framework.teacherMoves: Display both boxes prominently at the front. Ask: 'Look carefully — what is the same about these two boxes, and what is different?' Wait 20 seconds for silent observation before calling on responses. After predictions are recorded, say: 'Keep that prediction — we are about to find out whether your physics reasoning is correct.' Avoid confirming or denying any prediction. Circulate to read written predictions; note any student who mentions centre of gravity or base of support — flag them to share during the Explain phase.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Anchoring prediction activates prior knowledge of centre of gravity (Lesson 3) and equilibrium states (Lesson 4). The think-pair structure ensures every student commits to a reasoned prediction before observation, preventing passive watching.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher scans written predictions for evidence that students connect centre of gravity height or base width to stability. Students who write only 'the heavier one falls' reveal a misconception about weight vs. turning effect that must be addressed during explanation.

framework.learnerExperience: INVESTIGATION A — Centre of Gravity Height (10 minutes): Groups of 4 receive: one tall cardboard box (approx. 30 cm x 10 cm x 10 cm), two equal lumps of clay (~100 g each), a protractor, and a ruler. Step 1 — Baseline: Stand the box empty on the desk; tilt it slowly until it just topples; record the critical angle using the protractor. Step 2 — Clay at top: Fix both clay lumps inside at the top of the box with tape; repeat the tilt test; record the critical angle. Step 3 — Clay at base: Move both clay lumps to the inside base; repeat; record. Students tabulate: Configuration | Critical tipping angle | Toppled easily? (Y/N). INVESTIGATION B — Base of Support Width (8 minutes): Each group receives a second identical empty box with four scored cardboard flaps at its base that can be folded outward to widen the footprint. Step 1 — Flaps folded in (narrow base, approx. 10 cm x 10 cm): Tilt until topple; record angle. Step 2 — Two flaps out (medium base, approx. 10 cm x 20 cm): Repeat. Step 3 — All four flaps out (wide base, approx. 10 cm x 30 cm): Repeat. Students record results in the same tabular format. CASE STUDY (3 minutes reading): Each pair receives a laminated case-study card titled 'Nairobi Construction Site — Tower Crane Incident, 2015'. The card describes (in student-accessible language) how a tower crane was loaded asymmetrically — a heavy concrete block was lifted and swung beyond the rated radius — shifting the effective centre of gravity outside the crane base, causing the structure to overturn. Two guided questions are printed on the card: (1) Using the word moment, explain why the crane toppled. (2) Suggest TWO design changes that could have prevented the toppling.
framework.teacherMoves: Before Investigation A begins, say: 'Your job is to change ONE thing at a time and keep everything else the same — that is what makes this a fair test. Tell me: what are you keeping the same?' Wait 15 seconds; accept responses. During the investigations, circulate and prompt with: 'Your critical angle went up from 12 degrees to 31 degrees when you moved the clay to the base — what does that tell you about the turning effect needed to topple it?' For groups who finish early, prompt: 'Can you sketch where the centre of gravity is in each configuration — and draw the vertical line through it to show when it crosses the base edge?' During case-study reading, prompt: 'Find the word radius in the card — what does radius mean in terms of the moment formula?' After all observations, call the class together and ask a group spokesperson to share one surprising result before moving to the Explain phase.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Back-to-back controlled investigations enforce the scientific practice of changing one variable at a time, making the causal claims ('lowering centre of gravity increases stability' and 'widening the base increases stability') directly observable rather than told. The case study bridges controlled lab findings to a real high-stakes engineering failure, advancing the storyline toward the driving question.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher checks data tables for correct identification of the independent variable in each investigation. Listening for the phrase 'the angle got bigger' is insufficient — teacher probes: 'Why did a bigger angle mean more stable?' to ensure students link angle to the vertical-line-outside-base criterion from Lesson 4.

framework.learnerExperience: Whole-class sense-making (8 minutes). The teacher draws a quick side-view diagram of the tall box on the board — first with clay at the top, then with clay at the base — marking the centre of gravity (x) and the base of support. Students are invited to come up and draw the vertical line through the centre of gravity at the critical tipping moment in each case. The class discusses: 'Which configuration needs the box to lean further before the vertical line exits the base?' Students verbalise: 'When the centre of gravity is lower, the box must tilt more before it topples — so it is more stable.' The same diagrammatic analysis is done for the wide-base vs. narrow-base box. Students then write a two-sentence causal explanation in their books: Sentence 1 links centre of gravity height to stability; Sentence 2 links base width to stability. The teacher introduces three Kenya-relevant engineering examples using a mini-poster or whiteboard sketches: (1) Double-decker Citi Hoppa buses in Nairobi — heavy engine and battery packs kept low, narrow upper deck; (2) Grain storage silos at the Eldoret cereal board — wide circular base, low centre of mass; (3) Traditional Turkana water-pot carriers — wide-brimmed pot base resting on a head ring, keeping the load centred over the carrier. For the case study, pairs share their two design-change suggestions; the teacher consolidates responses on the board under two headings: 'Lower the centre of gravity' and 'Widen the base of support', reinforcing that engineers use the same two strategies students just discovered in the lab.
framework.teacherMoves: When drawing the diagram, use a think-aloud: 'I am placing the x for centre of gravity higher up here — now watch what happens to the vertical line as I tilt the box.' Pause 10 seconds after drawing each vertical line and ask: 'Has the line left the base yet? No? So has it toppled?' After students write their two sentences, cold-call three students and ask them to read their sentence aloud. If a student says 'heavier base makes it stable,' redirect: 'Is it the mass that matters, or where the mass is placed — and why?' Reference the Principle of Moments from Lesson 2: 'Remember — moment equals force times perpendicular distance. When the centre of gravity shifts outside the base, the weight itself creates a toppling moment. Heavier is not the same as more stable.' Connect back to the anchoring phenomenon explicitly: 'So now we can explain the Jiko stool — which of these two strategies did the wider four-legged stool use?'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Diagram annotation by students (not just the teacher) ensures active sense-making. The two-sentence writing task is a low-stakes formative writing move that forces each student to articulate a causal claim. Clustering the case-study suggestions under the same two headings students discovered in the lab closes the loop between investigation and application.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher reads the two written sentences for causal language. Red flag: sentences that say 'lower centre of gravity means the object is heavier at the bottom' — this conflates mass distribution with weight, requiring a follow-up probe. Green flag: sentences that use the vertical-line-through-centre-of-gravity criterion explicitly.

framework.learnerExperience: Students receive one green sticky note each. The teacher directs them: 'Write one piece of NEW EVIDENCE from today that helps answer the driving question — Why do some objects stay balanced while others topple?' Students write individually (2 minutes), then come up and place their note on the DQB in the section labelled Lesson 5 Evidence. Two or three notes are read aloud. The teacher also draws attention to any Lesson 1 or Lesson 2 sticky-note questions that can now be fully answered (e.g., 'Does a wider stool always win?' — YES, because a wider base means the vertical line through the centre of gravity must travel further before leaving the base of support). The teacher posts one new orange prompt card: 'Can we now PREDICT, before building anything, whether a design will topple or stay stable — and can we calculate it?' This bridges to Lesson 6 consolidation.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Today you moved from describing stability to explaining it and now to applying it. Your green note should show that shift — do not write what you did; write what it proves.' After notes are posted, ask: 'Which Lesson 1 question on the board can someone now answer using today and all our previous lessons?' Wait 20 seconds. Affirm correct answers and mark answered questions with a tick. Leave unanswered questions visible for Lesson 6.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: The DQB move makes the progressive accumulation of evidence visible and narrative — students see their own questions getting answered over time, reinforcing the storyline coherence and metacognitive awareness of their own learning trajectory.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher reads green sticky notes for evidence of transfer: does the student cite a specific observation (angle, clay position, flap width) or only a vague claim? Vague notes signal that the student has not yet connected observation to explanation and will need targeted support in Lesson 6.

framework.learnerExperience: Students open their cumulative model — the annotated Jiko stool diagram maintained since Lesson 1. Working in pairs, they add two new layers: (1) Stability-factor labels — two arrows or callout boxes, one pointing to the stool base with the label 'Wider base — vertical line through centre of gravity must travel further to exit base — MORE STABLE', and one pointing to the seat surface with the label 'Load kept low — centre of gravity stays low — MORE STABLE'; (2) Engineering example inset — a small sketch in the corner of either a Citi Hoppa double-decker bus or a construction crane, labelled with the two stability strategies. Students also update the Stool-vs-Jerrycan scenario: they annotate where the centre of gravity shifts when the jerrycan is placed on the corner and add a note explaining why the narrow stool topples but the wide stool does not. Pairs share their updated models with an adjacent pair and offer one piece of specific feedback using the sentence starter: 'Your model clearly shows _________ but could add _________ to make the causal chain clearer.'
framework.teacherMoves: Circulate and check that students are adding CAUSAL labels (not just descriptive labels). Prompt: 'Your arrow points to the base — what does the wider base actually do to the vertical line through the centre of gravity? Can you add those words?' For the engineering inset, ask: 'Which of the two strategies does a double-decker bus use — lowering centre of gravity, widening base, or both — and where exactly?' During peer feedback, listen for whether feedback is specific and physics-based. If it is vague (e.g., 'it looks good'), prompt: 'Can you find one place where the causal explanation is missing — where it says what happened but not why?' Before the lesson ends, ask one pair to hold up their model and describe one update to the class in 30 seconds.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative model building is the core epistemic practice of the unit — each lesson deposits a new explanatory layer, so by Lesson 6 the model is a complete, student-constructed explanation of the driving question. Peer feedback with a sentence starter scaffold ensures feedback is substantive rather than social.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher photographs three to four models (with student permission) to project at the start of Lesson 6 as anchor examples. Assessment criterion: Does the model show the vertical-line-through-centre-of-gravity mechanism explicitly, or only name the two factors without explaining the mechanism? The latter is the key gap to close in Lesson 6.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, ask: (1) Did students successfully isolate one variable at a time in both investigations, or did groups inadvertently change two variables simultaneously? If yes, plan a brief fair-test review at the start of Lesson 6. (2) Were students able to articulate the vertical-line-through-centre-of-gravity mechanism in their written sentences, or did they revert to describing observations? If the latter, consider projecting two contrasting student sentences at the start of Lesson 6 (anonymised) and asking the class to identify which is causal and which is merely descriptive. (3) Did the case-study card generate genuine engagement with the engineering context, or did it feel like a worksheet add-on? If engagement was low, in Lesson 6 invite students to suggest a local Kenyan infrastructure project (e.g., the Nairobi Expressway bridge piers, the Standard Gauge Railway viaducts) and ask how the same principles apply. (4) Check cumulative models photographed — are the stability-factor labels causal or merely descriptive? Use these as diagnostic data for targeted support in Lesson 6 consolidation.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: When clay was added to the TOP of the tall box, the critical tipping angle was small (the box toppled easily). When clay was moved to the BASE, the critical tipping angle was much larger (harder to topple). When cardboard flaps widened the base, the critical tipping angle increased further. In the case study, the crane toppled when its load was swung beyond the rated radius, shifting the effective centre of gravity outside the base.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Stability increases when the centre of gravity is lowered and when the base of support is widened. An object topples when the vertical line through its centre of gravity falls outside the base of support. Engineers apply both strategies deliberately in crane design, double-decker bus geometry, silo construction, and load distribution.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: In the Jiko stool phenomenon, the narrow three-legged stool toppled when the jerrycan was placed at the corner because the combined centre of gravity of stool-plus-load shifted far to one side, and the tall load kept the centre of gravity high — so only a small nudge moved the vertical line outside the narrow base. The wide four-legged stool survived because its broad base meant the vertical line had to travel much further before exiting the base of support, even with the jerrycan at the corner.

LESSONS.title: Can We Explain and Predict Toppling? — Consolidation, Model Finalisation, and DQB Review
LESSONS.aresKeywords: moment of a force, principle of moments, centre of gravity, base of support, stable equilibrium, unstable equilibrium, neutral equilibrium, toppling condition, perpendicular distance, stability factors

slo.purpose: To consolidate all learning from the sub-strand by using the completed cumulative model to fully explain the anchoring phenomenon, answer every Driving Question Board question with evidence, and demonstrate mastery through a summative problem set.
slo.knowledge: Students will be able to: (1) state and apply the formula Moment = Force x Perpendicular Distance; (2) state the Principle of Moments and apply it to predict balance or toppling; (3) define centre of gravity and explain how its position relative to the base of support determines the state of equilibrium; (4) distinguish stable, unstable, and neutral equilibrium with physical reasoning; (5) explain how lowering the centre of gravity and widening the base of support each increase stability.
slo.skills: Constructing and interpreting scientific models; quantitative problem solving involving moments; peer assessment using a co-constructed rubric; evidence-based scientific explanation; group presentation and scientific communication.
slo.attitudes: Appreciation of the relevance of Physics in everyday Kenyan life; confidence in using physical principles to predict real-world outcomes; collaborative respect during peer assessment; curiosity about engineering and design careers.
slo.keyInquiry: How do forces determine the stability and equilibrium of objects we encounter every day? — addressed fully by synthesising moment calculations, centre of gravity location, and stability principles to explain and predict toppling of the Jiko stool and related objects.
slo.purposeInStoryline: This is the final lesson of the storyline. Students bring together every concept built across Lessons 1 to 5 — turning effects, the Principle of Moments, centre of gravity, states of equilibrium, and stability factors — into one coherent explanation of the anchoring phenomenon. The Driving Question Board is closed with evidence-based answers, and a summative task confirms individual mastery before the sub-strand ends.
slo.safetyNotes: No hazardous chemicals. If physical props (stool, jerrycan) are used for demonstration, ensure the demonstration area is clear of students. Supervise any stacking of objects. Students with physical disabilities should be seated with clear sightlines to demonstration and presentation areas.

LESSONS.overview: In this 40-minute consolidation lesson students begin by revisiting the anchoring phenomenon — the Jiko stool and the jerrycan — and individually predict, using precise physical language, whether a modified scenario will topple or remain stable. They then observe a teacher-led re-enactment or short video clip of the phenomenon and compare outcomes with their predictions. In the Explain phase each group presents their finalised cumulative model, justifying every annotation with evidence from Lessons 1 to 5 and receiving structured peer feedback. The Driving Question Board is reviewed collectively: every student question posted across the unit is answered aloud with evidence. The lesson closes with an individual summative problem set — including a jerrycan-on-stool moment calculation — that assesses all five learning outcomes. Career connections (structural engineering, physiotherapy, aircraft loading, crane operation) are woven throughout. The lesson is explicitly tied to KICD key inquiry questions about the relevance of Physics in daily life and the role of forces in stability.

framework.learnerExperience: Students are shown a projected image of two stools side by side: the original three-legged Jiko stool (tall, narrow base) and the wider four-legged stool from the anchoring phenomenon. A new scenario is added — a maize sack (approximately 90 kg) placed at the far left corner of the three-legged stool, and a second scenario where the same sack is placed at the centre of the wide stool. Students individually write a prediction card (provided index card) answering three prompts: (1) Will each arrangement topple or remain stable? (2) What is the deciding physical condition? (3) If I lower the maize sack to the ground level (i.e., lower centre of gravity of the loaded system) would your answer change — and why? Students have 4 minutes of silent individual thinking. They then share with their partner using a Think-Pair routine. Predictions are NOT yet confirmed — they are posted on a designated Prediction Wall strip.
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher projects the two-stool image and reads each prompt aloud clearly. Teacher says: 'You have spent five lessons building the tools to answer this. Do not guess — reason. Use the words moment, centre of gravity, base of support, and toppling condition in your written prediction.' WAIT TIME: Allow 3 full minutes of silent individual writing before any partner talk begins — teacher times this visibly on a phone or watch and does not speak during the silence. Teacher circulates and reads over shoulders without commenting. After partner sharing, teacher calls on two pairs — one who predicted toppling and one who predicted stability for the same scenario — and says: 'Interesting. We have two different predictions for the same stool. Let us observe and find out who the evidence supports.' Teacher does NOT reveal the answer yet.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Activating prior knowledge through structured prediction prompts; Think-Pair routine surfaces misconceptions before the lesson proceeds; posting predictions publicly creates accountability for revision after observation.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher reads prediction cards over students shoulders during silent time. Target check: do students use the phrase vertical line through the centre of gravity falls outside the base of support rather than vague language like it is heavy or it will fall? Cards with vague language are flagged mentally for targeted questioning during the Explain phase. No marks are given at this stage.

framework.learnerExperience: Teacher or a student volunteer performs a live demonstration at the front: (A) places a filled 5-litre container (or equivalent improvised mass) at the far corner of the three-legged stool model (can be a small wooden stool replica or a cardboard model built in a previous lesson) and gives a gentle lateral nudge — students observe toppling; (B) places the same container at the centre of the wide flat model — students observe it rocks and recovers; (C) places the container at the corner of the wide flat model — students observe it is close to the tipping threshold. Students record observations in their notebooks in a three-column table: Scenario | What I observed (exactly) | Matches my prediction (yes/partial/no). Teacher then plays a 90-second projected animation or hand-drawn diagram sequence showing how the vertical line through the combined centre of gravity shifts as the load moves outward — crossing or not crossing the base of support boundary. Students annotate their observation table with a fourth column: Physical reason (using unit vocabulary). The observation phase takes approximately 7 minutes total.
framework.teacherMoves: Before the demonstration teacher says: 'Watch the moment the stool begins to tilt. Ask yourself — where is the vertical line through the centre of gravity at that exact instant?' WAIT TIME: After the toppling demonstration, teacher pauses for 20 seconds of silence and says nothing — allowing students to process what they saw before asking: 'Turn to your prediction card. Without erasing, write the word REVISED if your prediction needs updating and explain in one sentence why the evidence changed your thinking.' Teacher then asks: 'Who had a partial match — you got the outcome right but for the wrong reason? Raise your hand.' Teacher acknowledges these students: 'That is excellent scientific honesty. The goal is to match both the outcome AND the reasoning.' Teacher uses the animation to ask: 'At which exact moment does the stool become committed to toppling — before the nudge, during, or after?' WAIT TIME: 15 seconds before taking responses.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Live demonstration anchors abstract concepts in direct sensory experience; the three-plus-one column table scaffolds disciplined observation rather than casual watching; the animation bridges observation and theory by making the invisible (centre of gravity line) visible; public prediction revision normalises science as an iterative process.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher scans REVISED annotations. If students write only 'because the centre of gravity moved' without specifying relative to the base of support, teacher makes a mental note and addresses this gap explicitly in the Explain phase introduction. Teacher asks one targeted question to the whole class: 'Give me the exact tipping condition in one sentence using all four of these words — vertical line, centre of gravity, base of support, outside.' Cold calls two students after 10 seconds of wait time.

framework.learnerExperience: Each group (4 to 5 students) displays their finalised cumulative model — the annotated diagram built progressively across all six lessons. Each model should show: force arrows and moment arms (Lesson 1); clockwise and anticlockwise moment labels with the balance condition (Lesson 2); centre of gravity marker on the stool (Lesson 3); stability state annotations (Lesson 4); stability factor labels and an engineering inset (Lesson 5); and now in Lesson 6 a final written explanation strip attached to the bottom of the model answering the driving question. Groups take turns (approximately 2 minutes per group) to present one chosen annotation and explain it to the class — they do not read it but explain it in their own words. Peers use a co-constructed peer assessment rubric (prepared on a half-sheet) with four criteria: (1) Scientific accuracy of explanation — is moment defined and applied correctly? (2) Use of evidence — is each claim linked to an observation or data from the unit? (3) Clarity of the model — can a newcomer understand it? (4) Connection to the anchoring phenomenon — does the explanation refer back to the Jiko stool? Each peer group awards 1 (developing), 2 (achieving), or 3 (exceeding) for each criterion and writes one warm (strength) and one wonder (question or suggestion). After presentations, the class collectively answers the driving question aloud: teacher scribes a class consensus answer on the board which students copy into their notebooks.
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher introduces the Explain phase by saying: 'Your model is not a decoration. It is a scientific argument. Every arrow, every label, every annotation must be something you can defend with evidence from our experiments. If a peer asks why, you must be able to answer.' During presentations teacher listens for three common errors and addresses them if they occur: (1) Confusing moment arm with total distance — teacher draws a perpendicular on the board and asks the class: 'Is this the right distance — yes or no?' WAIT TIME 10 seconds. (2) Saying the centre of gravity is always at the geometric centre — teacher holds up the cut-out Kenya map from Lesson 3 and asks: 'Is the centre of gravity of this shape at its geometric centre? What does your experiment from Lesson 3 tell you?' (3) Defining stable equilibrium only as returning to position without mentioning the vertical line condition — teacher prompts: 'What must be true about the vertical line for that return to happen?' After all presentations teacher scribes the class consensus answer to the driving question: 'Objects topple when the vertical line through the combined centre of gravity falls outside the base of support, producing an unbalanced moment that rotates the object away from equilibrium. Stability is increased by lowering the centre of gravity and widening the base of support, as engineers deliberately design in cranes, buses, and buildings.' Teacher then reviews the Driving Question Board: reads each posted question aloud, asks the class for the evidence-based answer, and physically moves or ticks each card. Teacher says for each resolved question: 'Which lesson gave us the evidence to answer this?' For any question the class cannot yet answer fully, teacher acknowledges it openly: 'That is a question that takes us beyond this sub-strand — it is a great starting point for further study.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Group model presentation externalises thinking and requires students to translate private understanding into public scientific language; co-constructed peer rubric develops metacognitive awareness of quality scientific explanation; collective DQB review provides narrative closure to the unit storyline; scribing the consensus answer models how scientists consolidate evidence into a shared claim.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher uses the peer rubric scores to identify groups scoring 1 on scientific accuracy. These groups are pulled aside during the summative problem set for a brief targeted re-teaching exchange. Teacher listens for whether students can connect each DQB question to a specific lesson and a specific piece of evidence — inability to do so reveals surface rather than deep understanding. Teacher notes which DQB questions remain unresolved for the class and plans a brief post-lesson follow-up if needed.

framework.learnerExperience: Although the DQB was created progressively from Lesson 1, this phase in Lesson 6 serves as the formal closure of the board. Students first do a silent gallery walk (2 minutes) around the room to re-read all cards posted since Lesson 1. Each student places a small green dot sticker on the card whose question they feel the unit answered most completely, and a small orange dot on any card they feel is still not fully answered for them personally. The class then gathers and the teacher leads a 4-minute structured discussion: three green-dot questions are read aloud and answered collectively with evidence; any orange-dot questions are discussed — if resolvable with unit knowledge, a student volunteer answers; if genuinely beyond the sub-strand, the teacher names the future topic (e.g., torque in rotational dynamics at A-level, structural engineering in technical training). Students add one final card to the DQB: a Career Connection card. Each student writes on a new card: 'A career that uses moment and stability principles is [CAREER] because [REASON LINKED TO PHYSICS].' Examples suggested by teacher if needed: physiotherapist (body centre of gravity in rehabilitation), structural engineer (building foundation design), aircraft load controller (load distribution for stable flight), Nairobi crane operator (safe load moments). Cards are posted and teacher reads three aloud, affirming the Physics-career link explicitly.
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher facilitates the gallery walk by saying: 'Read every card as if you are a scientist checking whether your community of learners has answered the question you posted weeks ago. Be honest with your dots.' WAIT TIME: The full 2 minutes of gallery walk are silent — teacher enforces this and does not narrate. During the structured discussion teacher says for each green-dot question: 'Before I ask for the answer, tell me — which experiment gave us the evidence? Name the lesson.' For orange-dot cards teacher says: 'If you still have this question, you are thinking like a scientist who knows the boundary of current knowledge. Here is where this question leads next in your Physics journey.' For Career Connection cards teacher says: 'Physics is not only in laboratories. Every matatu driver who loads passengers evenly, every mama mboga who balances a tray on her head, every JKIA aircraft engineer who checks load sheets — they are all applying what you have learned. Your future career will almost certainly touch these ideas.' Teacher posts own Career Connection card: 'A Physics teacher uses the principle of moments every time they decide where to stand relative to a stubborn classroom door hinge.' This models humour and self-inclusion.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Green and orange dot voting makes collective and individual knowledge gaps visible without shame; career connection cards bridge Physics content to student identity and future aspirations; the teacher modelling a career card reduces hierarchy and builds classroom community; naming future learning destinations respects students intellectual curiosity without leaving questions unresolved.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher photographs the DQB with orange dots for personal records — clusters of orange dots on specific questions signal topics needing re-teaching or additional resources in future iterations of the unit. Teacher listens to career connection reasoning: statements that say only 'engineers use Physics' without a specific moment or stability link indicate surface-level career awareness and are gently probed with 'How specifically does the concept of toppling show up in that job?'

framework.learnerExperience: Students complete the final update to their cumulative model by attaching a Explanation Strip — a written paragraph (minimum 5 sentences) at the bottom of their diagram. The strip must: (1) state when an object will topple (toppling condition); (2) reference the anchoring phenomenon specifically (the Jiko stool and the jerrycan); (3) use the terms moment, centre of gravity, base of support, stable equilibrium, and perpendicular distance each at least once; (4) include one quantitative example — students calculate the moment of the 20-litre jerrycan (mass 20 kg, g = 10 N/kg, pivoting about the nearest stool leg, perpendicular distance estimated as 0.3 m) and state whether this moment is balanced or unbalanced given the counteracting moment of the stool weight (stool mass 3 kg, centre of gravity 0.15 m from the same leg). Students then complete the individual summative problem set (5 questions printed on a half-sheet). Question 1: Define moment of a force and state its SI unit. Question 2: A student pushes a school gate with a force of 40 N at 0.8 m from the hinge. Calculate the moment. Question 3: A see-saw has a child of weight 300 N sitting 1.2 m from the pivot. Where must a second child of weight 400 N sit for the see-saw to balance? Question 4: Explain why a double-decker bus is designed with a heavy lower deck and a lighter upper deck. Use the terms centre of gravity and base of support. Question 5 (the jerrycan-on-stool problem): A 20-litre jerrycan of water (weight 200 N) is placed 0.4 m from the pivot leg of a three-legged stool. The stool weighs 30 N and its centre of gravity is 0.1 m on the other side of the pivot leg. (a) Calculate the clockwise moment of the jerrycan. (b) Calculate the anticlockwise moment of the stool weight. (c) State whether the stool topples or remains stable and justify your answer using the Principle of Moments. After completing the problem set individually, students write a personal Reflection Prompt in their notebooks: 'Before this unit I thought balance and toppling were about [OLD IDEA]. Now I understand that [NEW UNDERSTANDING] because [EVIDENCE FROM AN EXPERIMENT WE DID].'
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher introduces the Explanation Strip task by saying: 'This strip is the scientific legacy of your model. If someone picks up your diagram in ten years, this paragraph must be enough for them to understand the physics of toppling without asking you a single question. Write it for that future reader.' Teacher circulates during individual summative work, ensuring no collaboration. Teacher reads over shoulders for Question 5 specifically — if students subtract moments rather than compare them, teacher points to the student work and says quietly: 'Which moment is larger? What does a larger net moment tell you about the direction of rotation?' — without giving the answer. WAIT TIME: After distributing the summative sheet, teacher allows 30 seconds of silent reading before students begin writing — this reduces anxiety and allows students to plan their approach. At the 5-minute mark teacher announces: 'You should be on Question 3 or 4 by now — manage your time.' At the end of the summative, teacher says: 'Write your reflection prompt. Be honest. The most powerful learning happens when you name exactly what changed in your thinking and exactly why.' Teacher closes the lesson by returning to the anchoring phenomenon one final time: 'The Jiko stool that puzzled you in Lesson 1 — you can now explain it precisely, calculate its moments, and redesign it to be safer. That is what Physics gives you: not just the answer, but the power to predict and control.' Teacher announces the next sub-strand topic briefly to maintain continuity.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: The Explanation Strip externalises and consolidates integrated understanding in written scientific language; the quantitative example in the strip requires students to bridge conceptual and mathematical understanding; the summative problem set provides individual accountability across all five learning outcomes; the Reflection Prompt develops metacognitive awareness of conceptual change; the teacher closing call-back to the anchoring phenomenon gives the unit narrative a satisfying and memorable closure.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher collects summative problem sets at the end of the lesson. Marking focus: Question 5c is the highest-order item — students must correctly compare 80 Nm (jerrycan moment: 200 x 0.4) with 3 Nm (stool countermoment: 30 x 0.1) and conclude toppling occurs because the clockwise moment is greater. Students who reach the correct numerical answer but justify using only qualitative language (it is heavy) score partial credit and are identified for feedback. Reflection prompts are scanned for evidence of genuine conceptual change — prompts that restate the new idea in the same vague language as the old idea (now I understand balance) indicate surface learning and trigger a follow-up conversation. Teacher records results in the continuous assessment log against each of the five knowledge outcomes in the SLO.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, reflect on the following: (1) Were students able to use the toppling condition — vertical line through centre of gravity falling outside the base of support — precisely and consistently, or did vague language persist despite five lessons of building? If vague language persisted, what scaffolding would have helped earlier in the unit? (2) Did the DQB orange dots cluster on any one concept — for example, the distinction between moment arm and total distance, or the location of centre of gravity in irregular objects? If so, which lesson in the sequence needs to be strengthened in the next iteration? (3) Did the summative problem set reveal a gap between students who can perform moment calculations and students who can interpret what those calculations mean for stability? If so, how can quantitative and conceptual tasks be better integrated in Lessons 1 to 5? (4) Were career connections made authentically — did students name specific Physics principles in their career cards — or were the connections generic? What additional real-world examples from the Kenyan context (Mombasa port crane operators, JKIA load planners, Nairobi high-rise structural engineers) would deepen the relevance? (5) Did the peer assessment rubric generate genuinely useful feedback or did peers inflate each other scores? Would anonymous peer assessment or a moderated sample have been more productive? (6) Did the lesson fit within 40 minutes? If not, which phase ran over and what adjustment is recommended — for example, could the gallery walk be shortened to 90 seconds or the summative reduced to 4 questions without losing coverage of all learning outcomes?

summaryTablePrompt.observed: In the demonstration I observed that the three-legged stool with the jerrycan placed at its far corner toppled when nudged, while the wider four-legged stool with the same load at its corner only rocked and returned to its upright position. I also observed from the animation that the vertical line through the centre of gravity crossed outside the base of support at the exact moment the narrower stool committed to toppling.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: I learned that an object topples when the vertical line through its combined centre of gravity falls outside the base of support, creating an unbalanced moment that rotates the object away from equilibrium. I also learned that stability is increased by lowering the centre of gravity and by widening the base of support, and that engineers deliberately use both strategies in structures such as cranes, double-decker buses, and grain silos. I can now calculate moments and use the Principle of Moments to predict whether a loaded object will topple or remain balanced.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: The Jiko stool topples because placing the heavy jerrycan at its far corner shifts the combined centre of gravity of the stool-plus-load system outward. When the vertical line through that combined centre of gravity moves beyond the edge of the base of support — the triangle formed by the three legs — the clockwise moment of the load about the nearest leg is greater than the anticlockwise moment of the rest of the stool. This net unbalanced moment rotates the stool until it falls. The wider stool does not topple because its larger base of support keeps the vertical line through the centre of gravity inside the triangle even when the load is near the edge, so the net moment remains balanced or self-correcting. The same physics explains why engineers lower the centre of gravity and widen the base in every structure that must resist toppling forces.

FINAL_EXPLANATION.subjectLabel: Physics | Grade 10 | Sub-Strand 1.5: Moments of Equilibrium
FINAL_EXPLANATION.instructions: This Final Explanation document is your opportunity to show everything you have learned across the six lessons in this unit. Your goal is to answer the driving question fully and precisely, using the physics vocabulary, concepts, and evidence you have gathered. Read each section prompt carefully, then write your response in the space provided. Use the exemplar responses as a guide for the level of detail and scientific reasoning expected. A strong Final Explanation connects every idea back to the anchoring phenomenon — the Toppling Jiko Stool — and uses real Kenyan examples to support your thinking. Write in full sentences, include diagrams where prompted, and show all calculations clearly.

sections.title: Section 1: The Anchoring Phenomenon — What Is Really Happening?
sections.prompt: Revisit the Toppling Jiko Stool phenomenon. In your own words, explain why the three-legged stool toppled when the 20-litre jerrycan was placed on one corner and nudged, but the wider, lower four-legged stool with the same jerrycan barely tilted and returned to upright. In your explanation, you must: (a) identify the turning force (moment) responsible for toppling, (b) explain the role of the base of support, and (c) explain the role of the centre of gravity. You do not need to use numbers here — focus on the physics concepts.
sections.exemplar: When the 20-litre jerrycan (roughly 20 kg, weight ≈ 200 N) was placed on the corner of the tall three-legged stool and a nudge was applied, the combined centre of gravity of the stool-plus-jerrycan system shifted until it moved outside the triangular base of support formed by the three legs. Once the centre of gravity was outside the base, the weight of the system created a net turning moment — a clockwise moment about the tipping edge (the leg nearest the jerrycan) — with no restoring anticlockwise moment to balance it. The stool therefore toppled. The wider, lower four-legged stool had a larger rectangular base of support. Even with the same jerrycan on one corner, the combined centre of gravity remained inside the wider base when nudged, so the weight produced a restoring moment — an anticlockwise moment about the tipping edge that pulled the stool back to upright. The key difference was not the force (the weight was identical) but the geometry: the width of the base and the height of the centre of gravity both determined whether the system was in stable or unstable equilibrium.

sections.title: Section 2: Moments of Force — Calculating Turning Effects
sections.prompt: A market trader in Gikomba Market, Nairobi, uses a uniform wooden plank (length 2.4 m, weight 30 N) balanced on a pivot at its midpoint as a makeshift display shelf. She places a 5 kg bunch of bananas 0.8 m to the left of the pivot and a bag of maize flour of unknown mass 0.6 m to the right of the pivot. (a) Calculate the moment of the bananas about the pivot. (b) Using the principle of moments, calculate the mass of the maize flour bag needed to keep the plank in equilibrium. (c) State the principle of moments clearly in your own words. Show all working and units.
sections.exemplar: (a) Weight of bananas = 5 kg × 10 N/kg = 50 N. Moment of bananas (anticlockwise) = Force × perpendicular distance = 50 N × 0.8 m = 40 N·m anticlockwise. (b) Principle of moments states that for a body in equilibrium, the sum of clockwise moments about any pivot equals the sum of anticlockwise moments about the same pivot. Let the mass of the maize flour bag = m. Weight of maize flour bag = m × 10 N/kg. Clockwise moment = (m × 10) × 0.6. Setting clockwise moments equal to anticlockwise moments: (m × 10) × 0.6 = 40 N·m → 6m = 40 → m = 40 ÷ 6 ≈ 6.67 kg. The maize flour bag must have a mass of approximately 6.67 kg. (c) The principle of moments states that when an object is in rotational equilibrium, the total turning effect (moment) trying to rotate it clockwise about any point is exactly equal to the total turning effect trying to rotate it anticlockwise about the same point. This means all the turning forces are perfectly balanced — there is no net tendency to rotate.

sections.title: Section 3: Centre of Gravity and Stability
sections.prompt: Explain the concept of centre of gravity and how its position determines whether an object is in stable, unstable, or neutral equilibrium. Use the Jiko stool and at least ONE other Kenyan example (such as a Maasai moran carrying a rungu, a bus on the Nairobi–Nakuru highway, or a cyclist balancing a load in Kisumu market) to illustrate each type of equilibrium. Include a clearly labelled diagram for each type of equilibrium showing the base of support, the centre of gravity, and the direction of the weight.
sections.exemplar: The centre of gravity (CG) of an object is the single point through which the entire weight of the object appears to act, regardless of the object's orientation. Its position depends on the distribution of mass — denser or heavier parts pull the CG towards them. Stable equilibrium: When a low, wide Jiko stool is tilted slightly, its CG rises and moves, but stays inside the base of support. The weight then produces a restoring moment that returns the stool to upright. A fully loaded matatu on the Nairobi–Nakuru highway with heavy luggage packed low in the boot is another example — its CG is low and well within the wide wheelbase, so it resists toppling on bends. [Diagram: wide base, CG shown low and inside the base, arrow for weight acting downward inside the base, restoring moment arrow shown.] Unstable equilibrium: The tall three-legged Jiko stool with the jerrycan on its corner is an example. When nudged, the CG moves outside the base of support and the weight creates an overturning moment — the stool falls further from equilibrium. A Maasai moran balancing his rungu vertically on one finger tip is in unstable equilibrium — the tiniest movement shifts the CG outside its tiny base and it falls. [Diagram: narrow base, CG shown high, arrow for weight acting outside the base, overturning moment arrow shown.] Neutral equilibrium: A sufuria (cooking pot) lying on its curved side on a flat floor is in neutral equilibrium. When rolled, the CG neither rises nor falls — it stays at the same height — so there is no restoring or overturning moment. The pot stays wherever it is placed. [Diagram: curved base, CG at constant height, no net moment arrow.]

sections.title: Section 4: Conditions for Equilibrium
sections.prompt: State and explain BOTH conditions necessary for an object to be in complete (static) equilibrium. Then apply both conditions to a real engineering or everyday Kenyan context: a sign board hanging outside a shop on Moi Avenue, Mombasa, supported by two vertical ropes attached to a horizontal beam. The sign has a weight of 120 N and is hung such that one rope is 0.3 m from the left end and the other is 0.3 m from the right end of a 1.2 m uniform beam (beam weight = 40 N). Calculate the tension in each rope. Show all working.
sections.exemplar: The two conditions for static equilibrium are: (1) Translational equilibrium — the sum of all forces acting on the object in every direction must equal zero (ΣF = 0). This means there is no net force causing the object to accelerate in any direction. (2) Rotational equilibrium — the sum of all moments (turning effects) about any point must equal zero (Στ = 0). This means there is no net turning effect causing the object to rotate. Applying both conditions to the signboard: Let tension in left rope = T₁ and tension in right rope = T₂. Total downward force = weight of beam + weight of sign = 40 N + 120 N = 160 N. The sign is hung at the centre (0.6 m from each end). Condition 1 (ΣF = 0, vertical): T₁ + T₂ = 160 N. Condition 2 (Στ = 0, taking moments about the left rope attachment point at 0.3 m from left end): Clockwise moments: Weight of beam acts at centre of beam = 0.6 m from left end, so distance from left rope = 0.6 − 0.3 = 0.3 m → moment = 40 × 0.3 = 12 N·m. Weight of sign acts at centre = distance from left rope = 0.3 m → moment = 120 × 0.3 = 36 N·m. Total clockwise moment = 12 + 36 = 48 N·m. Anticlockwise moment: T₂ acts at right rope, 0.6 m from left rope → moment = T₂ × 0.6. Setting equal: T₂ × 0.6 = 48 → T₂ = 80 N. From Condition 1: T₁ = 160 − 80 = 80 N. Both ropes carry equal tension of 80 N because the sign is centred and the beam is uniform — confirming the symmetry of the setup.

sections.title: Section 5: Answering the Driving Question
sections.prompt: Now answer the driving question directly and completely: 'Why do some objects stay balanced while others topple — and how can we use the physics of turning forces to predict and control whether an object will be stable or fall?' Your answer must: (a) connect the physics of moments, centre of gravity, and conditions for equilibrium into one coherent explanation; (b) explain at least TWO design strategies engineers or everyday Kenyans use to improve stability; and (c) reflect on what surprised you or changed your thinking during this unit.
sections.exemplar: Whether an object stays balanced or topples is determined by the interplay of three connected ideas: turning forces (moments), the location of the centre of gravity, and the conditions for equilibrium. A moment is the turning effect of a force about a pivot, calculated as M = F × d. For an object to remain balanced, two conditions must be met simultaneously — the net force on it must be zero AND the net moment about any point must be zero. The centre of gravity is crucial because it is the point through which the object's entire weight acts. If the vertical line through the centre of gravity falls within the base of support, the weight produces a restoring moment when the object is tilted — it returns to upright (stable equilibrium). If it falls outside the base, the weight produces an overturning moment and the object topples. This is precisely what happened with the Jiko stool: the tall, narrow stool's combined centre of gravity (stool plus jerrycan) was pushed outside its small triangular base of support by the nudge, creating an unbalanced clockwise moment that caused it to topple. The wider, lower stool's centre of gravity stayed inside its larger rectangular base, so a restoring moment brought it back upright. We can use this physics to predict and control stability through deliberate design: (1) Lowering the centre of gravity — engineers design Kenyan matatus and buses with heavy engines and chassis low to the ground and warn against overloading roof racks, because a lower CG means greater stability on the winding roads of the Rift Valley escarpment. (2) Widening the base of support — a three-legged Jiko stool is less stable than a four-legged stool with legs spread wide; similarly, a Maasai moran spreads his feet apart when carrying a heavy load to widen his base. Both strategies increase the range of tilt before the CG escapes the base, making toppling far less likely. What surprised me most in this unit was realising that toppling has nothing to do with how heavy the object is — a light but top-heavy object topples far more easily than a heavy but low object. It is all about geometry and the position of the centre of gravity relative to the base of support.

rubric.excellent: Accurately defines and applies moments (M = F × d), the principle of moments, and both conditions for static equilibrium. All key terms (moment, pivot, equilibrium, centre of gravity, base of support) are used correctly and consistently throughout. Explains the distinction between stable, unstable, and neutral equilibrium with precision.
rubric.proficient: Correctly defines and applies most concepts. Minor errors in terminology or one condition for equilibrium is incompletely stated. Distinguishes between types of equilibrium but with limited depth.
